EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported estimated 70 mph wind gusts and tree damage.

In the aftermath of a large, impactful storm system that moved across the region on April 22-23, 2022, a tight pressure gradient resulted in isolated non-thunderstorm high winds across portions of southeast South Dakota during April 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph most consistently during the early morning hours, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System at the Platte-Winner Bridge (station SD554).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of April 23, 2022 within an area of elevated instability mainly along and east of Interstate 29. A couple thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of April 23, 2022 within an area of elevated instability mainly along and east of Interstate 29. A couple thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail.
A couple clusters of severe storms moved east and northeastward out of south central South Dakota, evolving into short bowing segments with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD589 2 miles east of Dixon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak synoptic-scale wave progressed through the area atop a flat upper-level ridge, initiating several clusters of thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.  Storms developed near a weak convergence zone, in an environment of 1000-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and marginally strong deep-layer shear of 30 knots.  Mainly multi-cell clusters resulted in marginally severe damaging wind gusts due to sub-cloud dryness and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four large metal grain bins were destroyed by thunderstorm wind gusts, but each of the damaged bins were empty at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of April 23, 2022 within an area of elevated instability mainly along and east of Interstate 29. A couple thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail.

Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly nickel size, with a few stones as large as ping-pong balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall on May 30 in northeast and east central South Dakota brought back minor flooding to upper portions of the Big Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall on May 30 across northeast and east central South Dakota resulted in a return to minor flood stage for the Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2).  A crest of 0.21 feet above flood stage was reached late on May 31. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

Dry weather resulted in a gradual drop in water levels along the James River through the month, and flooding ended during early- to mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of August 2022, with the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) at 0.45 feet above flood stage on August 1. A significant amount of rural land was flooded until the river dropped below flood stage on August 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive ridge of high pressure aloft forced a strong warm plume to the northeast, ahead of a frontal boundary on August 5.  The combination of temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and dew point readings in the mid 60s to around 70 led to heat index values from 105-110 for several hours across south central and southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 90s and dew point readings in the mid to upper 60s resulted in heat index values as high as 107 degrees near Ethan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms developed in the overnight warm advection zone ahead of an advancing cold front. Fairly prevalent warm and dry air aloft produced an environment favorable for wind gusts, some of which occurred as showers and thunderstorms weakened in the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wake low developed with a small area of damaging non-thunderstorm winds in and near Tyndall.  A South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 5 miles east-southeast of Tyndall measured a wind gust of 68 mph, and several tree limbs were reported down in Tyndall by the cooperative weather observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With elevated CAPE of 2500-3000 J/kg and minimal wind shear, rapidly developing updrafts resulted in periodic large hail and spotty damaging wind as storms moved along the Missouri River.
With elevated CAPE of 2500-3000 J/kg and minimal wind shear, rapidly developing updrafts resulted in periodic large hail and spotty damaging wind as storms moved along the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small branches were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated from 55 to 60 mph.  Some dime size hail accompanied the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone thunderstorm developed along a weak low-level convergence zone which featured around 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear around 40 knots. An isolated supercell developed and produced considerable hail, and occasional large hail.

A strengthening area of low pressure over Minnesota brought light snow and strong northwest winds to northeastern South Dakota from the evening of the 4th through the morning hours of the 5th. The combination of snowfall amounts of an inch or two, along with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts, caused blizzard conditions, especially in open country areas.

An Arctic high pressure brought bitterly cold temperatures from the evening of January 4th into the morning hours of January 7th. High temperatures dropped from the single digits and teens above zero on the 5th, to the single digits on either side of zero on the 6th. The coldest wind chills of 40 below to 50 degrees below zero occurred during the morning hours on January 6th. Some of the coldest recorded wind chill readings include 50 degrees below zero 4 miles west of Trail City, 49 degrees below zero 4 miles southwest of Hillhead, 10 miles west of Long Lake, 48 degrees below zero at Lake City, and 47 degrees below zero 3 miles south of Webster and 1 mile south of Gettysburg.

Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -40F at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD508 3 miles south-southwest of Beresford.

An Arctic high pressure brought bitterly cold temperatures from the morning of the 1st through the morning of the 2nd, having started in late December the previous calendar year (see the December 2021 Storm Data entry for additional information). High temperatures on the 1st were in the single digits below zero, making it one of the coldest January 1st on record for several locations. Wind chill values ranged from 30 below to 50 degrees below zero, with some improvement during the afternoon of the 1st. Some of the coldest recorded wind chill readings include 48 degrees below zero 4 miles SW of Hillhead, 46 degrees below zero miles west of Long Lake, 45 degrees below zero near Summit and in Lake City, and 44 degrees below zero 5 miles WNW of McLaughlin.

A warm front moved across western South Dakota during the early morning hours, accompanied by light precipitation, mostly in the form of freezing rain. Most of the precipitation fell across the northwestern into west central South Dakota plains, where  freezing rain amounts of a hundredth to near a tenth of an inch occurred. This coating of ice resulted in very hazardous travel during the early morning hours, with numerous accidents and Interstate 90 being closed east of Rapid City for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.

Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -37F at site KMHE near Mitchell and -35F at a personal weather station in Ethan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic high pressure brought bitterly cold temperatures from the morning of the 1st through the morning of the 2nd, having started in late December the previous calendar year (see the December 2021 Storm Data entry for additional information). High temperatures on the 1st were in the single digits below zero, making it one of the coldest January 1st on record for several locations. Wind chill values ranged from 30 below to 50 degrees below zero, with some improvement during the afternoon of the 1st. Some of the coldest recorded wind chill readings include 48 degrees below zero 4 miles SW of Hillhead, 46 degrees below zero miles west of Long Lake, 45 degrees below zero near Summit and in Lake City, and 44 degrees below zero 5 miles WNW of McLaughlin.

A warm front moved across western South Dakota during the early morning hours, accompanied by light precipitation, mostly in the form of freezing rain. Most of the precipitation fell across the northwestern into west central South Dakota plains, where  freezing rain amounts of a hundredth to near a tenth of an inch occurred. This coating of ice resulted in very hazardous travel during the early morning hours, with numerous accidents and Interstate 90 being closed east of Rapid City for a few hours.

An upper level system moved across the Northern Plains, bringing rain and snow to portions of western South Dakota. Across the northern Black Hills, occasional snow developed, with a period of heavier upslope-enhanced snow during the morning hours of the 24th. Snowfall amounts were highest across the higher elevations and northwest facing slopes, especially from the Cheyenne Crossing to O'Neill Pass, where three to seven inches were reported. A few inches of snow fell over the northern Black Hills into western portions of the central Black Hills.

Low pressure tracking across southern Canada generated winds of 50 to 60 mph. These strong winds combined with dry conditions to help generate a fire that burned a path approximately 12 miles long and 1/2 to 1 and 1/2 miles wide for a total 4556 acres burned. 13 departments responded to the fire, and efforts to suppress the fire were aided by local residents with farm equipment. The fire damaged a fire truck and tractor before being extinguished.

Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21.  In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 3 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon of February 21.  Precipitation changed to light snow, and while amounts were not significant with 1.5 inches at Bridgewater and 1.0 inch 5 miles northeast of Salem, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 46 mph near Montrose and 36 mph at Canova, visibility was occasionally one mile or less and briefly less than one-half mile between 0600-1000CST February 22.  In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21.  In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 3 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon and early evening of February 21. Slick roads caused a two-car accident in the northern portion of Sioux Falls which resulted in the fatality (indirect) of one driver. Precipitation changed to light snow, and while amounts were not significant with between 1.5 and 2.5 inches around Sioux Falls and 1.5 inches at Colton, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 45 mph near Dell Rapids and 40 mph at Sioux Falls, visibility was occasionally one mile or less and briefly less than one-half mile between 0700-1000CST February 22.  In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal passage was followed by strong winds and falling temperatures on the morning of February 1st. Sustained winds topped out around 40 mph at a few sites, with a peak wind of 67 mph in the Hillhead area on the northern tip of the Sisseton Hills. This event began late on the 31st of January.

Light freezing rain moved across parts of eastern South Dakota on Saturday evening. Icy roads resulted in several injury accidents southeast of Watertown, particularly along Interstate 29 and Highway 15. Three separate crashes were rollovers with non-life threating injuries. The incident involved multiple law enforcement agencies and required emergency services from neighboring counties.

A clipper system generated high winds but brought little to no precipitation to the forecast area. Dry conditions and winds peaking at 68 mph in eastern Edmunds County with similarly high gusts across the James Valley resulted in blowing dust. These same winds were also responsible for a vehicle rollover in Day county.  Even drier conditions in central South Dakota combined with winds that peaked around 55 mph, resulting in the spread of a nearly 500 acre sized grass fire 7 miles southwest of Murdo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These winds produced zero visibility in dust between 1200CST and 1300CST.

A band of moderate to heavy snow developed within a very cold airmass, setting up across north central South Dakota on the afternoon of February 20th and followed into the far northeast of the state on the morning of February 21st. This was part of a broader arctic blast that also featured dangerous cold and wind chills as well as an additional round of snow with strong winds and intermittent blizzard conditions. Peak snow amounts with this initial band of snow range between 12 inches in Kenel, 11 inches in Victor, 8.5 inches measured in Pollock, and 8 inches in Hecla, SD.

Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21.  In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 3 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon and early evening of February 21. Precipitation changed to light snow, and while amounts were not significant with 1.5 to 2.0 inches on the south side of Sioux Falls and 1.2 inches in Canton, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 41 mph near Davis and 32 mph at Canton, visibility was occasionally one mile or less between 0800-1200CST February 22.  In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: While snowfall was not significant by amount with 1.0 inches near Platte and 11 miles south of Emery, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 37 mph at Parkston and Wagner, visibility was occasionally one mile or less between 0900-1500CST February 22.  In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -30F.

A band of moderate to heavy snow developed within a very cold airmass, setting up across north central South Dakota on the afternoon of February 20th and followed into the far northeast of the state on the morning of February 21st. This was part of a broader arctic blast that also featured dangerous cold and wind chills as well as an additional round of snow with strong winds and intermittent blizzard conditions. Peak snow amounts with this initial band of snow range between 12 inches in Kenel, 11 inches in Victor, 8.5 inches measured in Pollock, and 8 inches in Hecla, SD.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon and early evening of February 21.  Precipitation changed to light snow, with 1.4 inches in Vermillion and 1.0 inch near Centerville. When combined with winds which gusted as high as 37 mph near Vermillion and 36 mph at Centerville, visibility was occasionally one mile or less between 0600-1200CST February 22.  In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a band of moderate to heavy snow moved through on February 20th into the 21st, periodic snowfall persisted across the area for much of February 21st and 22nd. Winds increased with peak gusts of 35 mph to 45 mph across far northeast South Dakota. Two to three day snow totals ranged from just a trace west of the Missouri Valley, to upwards of 15.5 inches along the state line in Roberts County. The fresh snow and persistent winds result in widespread near blizzard to blizzard conditions for parts of northeast South Dakota, and lead to the closure of interstate 29 from Watertown north to the state line.

A clipper system generated high winds but brought little to no precipitation to the forecast area. Dry conditions and winds peaking at 68 mph in eastern Edmunds County with similarly high gusts across the James Valley resulted in blowing dust. These same winds were also responsible for a vehicle rollover in Day county.  Even drier conditions in central South Dakota combined with winds that peaked around 55 mph, resulting in the spread of a nearly 500 acre sized grass fire 7 miles southwest of Murdo.

Gusty winds of 40 to 60 mph behind a cold front, combined with snow shower development, resulted in snow squalls producing very low visibilities and dangerous driving conditions on the adjacent plains north and east of Rapid City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. Snow squalls produced very low visibilities and dangerous driving conditions over portions of the Rapid City area, especially northern and eastern portions of the city. Several accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty northerly winds and helped to produce upslope enhanced snowfall across the northern Black Hills area. Snowfall amounts around six inches were reported across the northern Black Hills area, especially the higher elevations west of Lead and Deadwood.

A large upper trough brought a couple of disturbances across the region, resulting in periods of snow across a good portion of western South Dakota. The first disturbance brought snow across northwestern South Dakota and a couple of bands of heavier snowfall through northern portions of Harding and Perkins Counties. Snowfall of two to four inches were common across northwestern South Dakota, with four to eight inches and locally higher amounts within the heavier bands close to the North Dakota border. The next disturbance brought snow mostly to southwestern into south central South Dakota, which produced mostly two to four inches across these areas.

Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21.  In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 3 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While snowfall was not significant by amount with 3.0 inches at Wessington Springs, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 34 mph at Wessington Springs, visibility was briefly one mile or less between 0800-1500CST February 22.  In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -35F.

An isolated high wind event occurred along the eastern side of the Prairie Coteau in Roberts County during the morning hours. Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph with peak gusts of 58 mph produced blowing and drifting snow, impacting travel during the morning commute along Interstate 29.
A single roll-over vehicle accident occurred on Interstate 29 due to icy roadways. Two occupants were transported to the hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -42F at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD530 5 miles south of Freeman, -37F at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD570 1 mile northwest of Tripp, and -33F at SDSU mesonet site PSTS2 at Parkston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cold air entrenched across the area, a mid-level wave dug southward through the Plains. A swath of snowfall and some light freezing rain developed in the leading warm advection zone northeast of the track of low pressure from South Dakota to Missouri. The highest snowfall of 5 to 11 inches was northeast of a line from De Smet, SD to Ida Grove, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief freezing rain resulted in less than a tenth of an inch ice accumulation. Snowfall was briefly moderate to heavy between 1200-1500CST and created treacherous travel conditions.  Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches 5 miles west of Bruce, 5.6 inches at Brookings and 4.5 inches 2 miles south southeast of Astoria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked southeastward from eastern Montana on the 14th into western Iowa on the evening of the 15th. The low pressure system brought a period of freezing rain and snow over northeastern South Dakota. Travel was impacted due to icy roadways and blowing snow resulting in several vehicle accidents. Some of the highest snowfall totals include 10 inches at Milbank, 9.4 inches four miles west of Sisseton, 9 inches eight miles east-northeast of Sisseton, and 8 inches at La Bolt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -37F at Road Weather Information System site SD504 4 miles south of Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moved slowly across the Northern Plains, producing a prolonged period of strong northwesterly winds across the area. The strongest winds developed during the early morning of the 6th and continued into the daytime hours of the 7th. Sustained winds of 30 to 50 mph and gusts of 60 to 80 mph were recorded at times, especially across the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains. Several tractor-trailers were blown over on Interstate 90 east of Rapid City.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over southern Oglala Lakota County and tracked northeast across portions of eastern Oglala Lakota, far southeastern Pennington, northeast Jackson, Haakon, and southeast Ziebach Counties. The storm produced large hail to golf ball size, but a few larger hailstones were reported in a couple of areas. Gusty winds also accompanied the storm.

A strong storm system tracked across the Northern Plains; bringing rain, snow, and a prolonged period of gusty winds to the area. The heaviest snow developed across far northwestern South Dakota, where up to a foot of snow fell. Strong winds over 60 mph on the back side of the storm produced blizzard conditions at times across much of Harding and Perkins Counties. Farther south across western Butte County into the northern Black Hills, snowfall amounts were up to six inches, with gusty winds producing some areas of blowing snow and reduced visibilities.

A strong spring storm brought heavy snow from the northern Black Hills to portions of northwestern South Dakota, with blizzard conditions across parts of the plains. Snowfall of five to ten inches were common across much of far northwestern South Dakota, with some higher amounts across Harding County. Strong winds produced significant blowing and drifting snow, with blizzard conditions at times. Across the northern Black Hills area, snowfall amounts were eight to 16 inches, with amounts over 20 inches over favored upslope areas of the northern Black Hills. Amounts tapered off to a few inches across the rest of northwestern into west central South Dakota, but strong winds with blowing and drifting snow caused hazardous travel at times.

A strong spring storm brought heavy snow from the northern Black Hills to portions of northwestern South Dakota, with blizzard conditions across parts of the plains. Snowfall of five to ten inches were common across much of far northwestern South Dakota, with some higher amounts across Harding County. Strong winds produced significant blowing and drifting snow, with blizzard conditions at times. Across the northern Black Hills area, snowfall amounts were eight to 16 inches, with amounts over 20 inches over favored upslope areas of the northern Black Hills. Amounts tapered off to a few inches across the rest of northwestern into west central South Dakota, but strong winds with blowing and drifting snow caused hazardous travel at times.

An intense low pressure system moved slowly across the Northern Plains, producing a prolonged period of strong northwesterly winds across the area. The strongest winds developed during the early morning of the 6th and continued into the daytime hours of the 7th. Several tractor-trailers were blown over on Interstate 90 east of Rapid City.

Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous branches around 3 inches in diameter were downed by thunderstorm winds. | A large dead tree of 20 inches in diameter toppled across a road. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With elevated CAPE of 2500-3000 J/kg and minimal wind shear, rapidly developing updrafts resulted in periodic large hail and spotty damaging wind as storms moved along the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While hail was mostly pea-size, there were several of quarter size mixed in, with a small area of total crop loss about one-half mile east. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened slightly to extreme (D3) during early August. Temperatures were two to three degrees above normal, with around 66 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through August. Temperatures were around two degrees above normal, with around 75 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near average with precipitation slightly below normal until the very end of the month, which led to continuation of severe (D2) drought conditions through May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions eased to moderate (D1) early June, with early month temperatures slightly below normal and rainfall around 50 percent of normal. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed during early August, then rapidly degraded to extreme (D3) by mid August. Temperatures were two to three degrees above normal, with around 66 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 65 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD601 3 miles south of Corsica. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability and a strengthening low-level jet developed a complex of thunderstorms which built southward into the Highway 14 corridor overnight. Isolated large hail fell with the strongest storm near De Smet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Tyndall was 25 to 50 percent of normal, with temperatures around 4 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) during the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 78 mph was measured by a personal weather station 2 miles west of Salem. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 80 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous one to two-inch branches were downed by thunderstorm winds. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep cyclonic vortex digging into Minnesota resulted in a very strong synoptic gradient across South Dakota on the southwest flank of the system.  Diurnal heating resulted in very strong winds, frequently as high as 40 to 50 mph, with some gusts to around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds were sustained from 40 to 50 mph at times during the later morning and afternoon hours. Peak wind gusts of 62 mph were measured at the South Dakota Road Weather Information site SD588 2 mile west-southwest of Pickstown, and 58 mph 4 miles east of Dixon at site SD589. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation near Chamberlain was less than 10 percent of normal, with temperatures of 3 to 4 degrees above normal during September. Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) at mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation and temperatures were near average, which allowed extreme (D3) drought to ease to severe (D2) drought in early May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at White Lake was less than 25 percent of normal, with temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal during September. Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) at mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through June, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was near average, but temperatures were a shade below normal for May. Areas of severe (D2) drought eased to moderate (D1) drought by late month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Wessington Springs was around 10 percent of normal, with temperatures of 4 to 5 degrees above normal during September. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed through the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures with precipitation from 50 to 90 percent of normal, led to the development of severe drought conditions in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 2 degrees above normal with 50 to 75 percent of normal precipitation led to the development of severe (D2) drought conditions by late July. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak synoptic-scale wave progressed through the area atop a flat upper-level ridge, initiating several clusters of thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.  Storms developed near a weak convergence zone, in an environment of 1000-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and marginally strong deep-layer shear of 30 knots.  Mainly multi-cell clusters resulted in marginally severe damaging wind gusts due to sub-cloud dryness and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed with a strengthening low-level jet during the overnight hours.  With MUCAPE of around 1000 J/kg and 30 knots of effective shear, isolated storms produced up to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts damaged several buildings in Howard, with numerous trees and powerlines down. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind-driven pea-sized hail caused damage to vegetation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 67 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD508 3 miles south-southwest of Beresford. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 79 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD603 1 mile west of Lane. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Huron and near Wessington was less than 10 percent of normal, with temperatures of 3 to 4 degrees above normal during September. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed through the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 43 mph at times during the late morning and early afternoon at a personal weather station 7 miles east-northeast of Wagner. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by Automated Surface Observing System site KMHE. A peak wind gust of 75 mph occurred at 1352CST. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe west of Spearfish, producing lots of small hail and a few stones around quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was covered with hail, mostly dime size or less.

Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions eased to moderate (D1) early June, with early month temperatures slightly below normal and rainfall around 50 percent of normal. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe over the southern Black Hills and tracked northeast across the eastern Black Hills and adjacent plains. The storm produced large hail around quarter size and eventually strong wind gusts as it moved onto the plains.

While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation and temperatures were near average, which allowed extreme (D3) drought to ease to severe (D2) drought by late May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures with below-normal precipitation led to the stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.

Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 80 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon and evening of April 22, 2022 and moved northward with an advancing surface warm front. A couple thunderstorms took on supercell characteristics near the surface boundary and produced large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter. A brief tornado also occurred near Wolsey, SD.||In the post warm frontal air mass from mid-evening into the early overnight of April 23, 2022, non-thunderstorm high winds produced multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds varied frequently between 40 and 53 mph. A peak wind gust of 71 mph occurred at 2010CST at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD589 4 miles east of Dixon, with 69 mph recorded at 1931CST at SD588 1 mile southwest of Pickstown. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abundant hail up to nickel-sized fell, which was enough to fill a five gallon bucket at the end of a downspout. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system produced several severe thunderstorms, high winds, a brief tornado, and freezing rain. The severe storms developed over south-central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 22nd and advanced northward along with a warm front into north-central and northeastern South Dakota into the early morning hours of the 23rd, bringing widespread damage to the area. The storms produced hail, up to two inches in diameter, 60 to 75 mph wind, and locally heavy rainfall. Strong thunderstorm winds rolled a camper on Highway 45 near Sunshine Bible Academy in Hand County. In addition, half-dollar size hail broke windows on a home seven miles southwest of Ridgeview, and two-inch hail broke vehicle and house windows near Ridgeview. Lightning caused some prairie and hay bale fires around the Highmore area. The center of the low-pressure system passed over Aberdeen on April 23rd at 12:20 AM CST, producing a mean sea level pressure of 983.5 mb. The pressure was just 3.5 mb shy of Aberdeen's 980 mb record for April. Additional severe storms developed during the afternoon hours of the 23rd. The storms produce small hail in Campbell County and a brief tornado in Edmunds County. High winds followed the back side of the surface low pressure during the late evening hours on April 22nd into the afternoon hours on April 23rd. Some of the highest wind reports include 79 mph at Herreid, 75 mph 5 miles east of Danforth, 69 mph at Webster, and 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Freezing rain in north-central South Dakota on the 23rd caused ice to accumulate on power lines. With ice accretion and high winds, many power lines went down, mainly in Corson and Dewey county. The storm system was the third significant storm system of April, which further delayed the agricultural planting season.

A strengthening area of low pressure over Minnesota brought high winds to central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota from the afternoon hours on Tuesday, April 5th, through the evening hours on April 7th. Some of the highest wind gusts include 65 mph 17 miles west of Polo and 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport and 64 mph 13 miles north of Vivian. The long-duration wind event caused sporadic tree damage and shingles to blow off of roofs. The Redfield Press reported roof damage to the Northwestern High School in Spink County with different materials, including metal, blown off the school. Light, accumulating snowfall combined with the very strong winds to produce periods of reduced visibility over northeastern South Dakota. Various activities were canceled or postponed, including area schools.

There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was measured by South Dakota State University mesonet site CTNS2 2 miles east-southeast of Colton. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022, with a slow fall in river levels. However, heavy rainfall which totaled 5 to 7 inches fell in a very small portion of the mid to upper-James River Basin during the first week of July and resulted in a mid- to late-month crest before another slow fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022 on the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2). Five to seven inches of rainfall the first seven days of July in a small area at and north of Huron resulted in a crest at 1.70 feet above flood stage on July 17. A significant amount of rural land was flooded. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Platte and near Academy was 25 to 50 percent of normal, with temperatures around 3 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near average with precipitation slightly below normal, which led to continuation of severe (D2) drought conditions through May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple clusters of severe storms moved east and northeastward out of south central South Dakota, evolving into short bowing segments with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metal roof panels were blown off a pole barn by thunderstorm winds. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain of the High Plains and moved into central South Dakota during the evening of June 29th. Despite weak instability, deep inverted-V soundings resulted in DCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg, allowing for isolated damaging wind gusts with the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed a large tree branch. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 71 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KYKN 2 miles northeast of Yankton. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed in early August, but was quickly abated by rainfall from 3 to 6 inches around August 7. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cool and wet period at the start of the month eased conditions from severe (D2) drought to moderate (D1) drought early in May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD605 3 miles east-southeast of Parker. A peak wind gust of 79 mph occurred at 1522CST. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through June, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low-pressure system brought damaging high winds, blizzard conditions, and areas of blowing dust from April 12th to April 14th. The prolonged period of high winds caused sporadic tree and shingle damage to roofs. In addition, the combination of ongoing drought conditions and the high winds caused blowing dust in south-central South Dakota. The Hand County Sheriff reported significantly reduced visibility due to blowing dust on the 14th. Some of the highest wind gusts include 73 mph 11 miles south of Bullhead, 72 mph in Tunerville, 71 mph 6 SSW of Peever, and 67 mph 2 miles north of the Pierre Airport and 10 miles SW of Long Lake.

Some of the highest wind reports include 79 mph at Herreid, 75 mph 5 miles east of Danforth, 69 mph at Webster, and 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Freezing rain in north-central South Dakota on the 23rd caused ice to accumulate on power lines. With ice accretion and high winds, many power lines went down, mainly in Corson and Dewey county. The storm system was the third significant storm system of April, which further delayed the agricultural planting season.
Routed water from heavier early-spring precipitation well to the north brought sections of the James River above flood stage for the first time in nearly two years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As water from early spring rains and late winter snowmelt in North Dakota and far northern South Dakota routed downstream, the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) climbed into minor flood stage on May 9, and reached 1.85 feet above flood stage at the end of the month. Significant amounts of agricultural land were inundated. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

A powerful low pressure system produced several severe thunderstorms, high winds, a brief tornado, and freezing rain. The severe storms developed over south-central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 22nd and advanced northward along with a warm front into north-central and northeastern South Dakota into the early morning hours of the 23rd, bringing widespread damage to the area. The storms produced hail, up to two inches in diameter, 60 to 75 mph wind, and locally heavy rainfall. Strong thunderstorm winds rolled a camper on Highway 45 near Sunshine Bible Academy in Hand County. In addition, half-dollar size hail broke windows on a home seven miles southwest of Ridgeview, and two-inch hail broke vehicle and house windows near Ridgeview. Lightning caused some prairie and hay bale fires around the Highmore area. The center of the low-pressure system passed over Aberdeen on April 23rd at 12:20 AM CST, producing a mean sea level pressure of 983.5 mb. The pressure was just 3.5 mb shy of Aberdeen's 980 mb record for April. Additional severe storms developed during the afternoon hours of the 23rd. The storms produce small hail in Campbell County and a brief tornado in Edmunds County. High winds followed the back side of the surface low pressure during the late evening hours on April 22nd into the afternoon hours on April 23rd. Some of the highest wind reports include 79 mph at Herreid, 75 mph 5 miles east of Danforth, 69 mph at Webster, and 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Freezing rain in north-central South Dakota on the 23rd caused ice to accumulate on power lines. With ice accretion and high winds, many power lines went down, mainly in Corson and Dewey county. The storm system was the third significant storm system of April, which further delayed the agricultural planting season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near average with precipitation slightly below normal until the very end of the month, which led to continuation of severe (D2) drought conditions through May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD570 one mile northwest of Tripp. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown was observed in an open field. The tornado caused no damage from which the NWS could assign an EF-scale rating. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms developed in the overnight warm advection zone ahead of an advancing cold front. Fairly prevalent warm and dry air aloft produced an environment favorable for wind gusts, some of which occurred as showers and thunderstorms weakened in the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD532 2 miles west-southwest of Gayville. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple clusters of severe storms moved east and northeastward out of south central South Dakota, evolving into short bowing segments with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 69 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD554 14 miles west of Platte. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple clusters of severe storms moved east and northeastward out of south central South Dakota, evolving into short bowing segments with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds to 65 mph were measured by a handheld anemometer. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inch limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds near downtown Canova. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.
