A powerful low pressure system brought damaging high winds, blizzard conditions, and areas of blowing dust from April 12th to April 14th. The combination of heavy snowfall and high winds produced blizzard conditions in Corson and Dewey counties in north-central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6.7 inches in McLaughlin, to 9 inches 14 miles NNE from Isabel, to 14 inches in Keldron. The snowfall and sustained winds of 45 mph with gusts exceeding 55 mph produce over six feet high snowdrifts. The blizzard caused the death of newborn livestock in north-central South Dakota. Mail was delayed in portions of north-central South Dakota as trucks could not leave Bismarck due to dangerous road conditions. The storm canceled many school activities in north-central South Dakota. In eastern South Dakota, snowfall amounts at or below one inch combined with high winds to produce blizzard conditions, especially in the open country areas. The blizzard significantly impacted travel - The Watertown Police Department recommended no travel advised within the city limits. There were additional reports of people pulled over in Clark County on Highway 212 and Interstate 29 between Watertown and South Shore due to zero visibility. Numerous school activities were canceled, and businesses closed early due to the horrific weather conditions. Some area schools were delayed or closed on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As many as seven calves were killed during this blizzard.

Above-normal temperatures with below-normal precipitation led to the stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions remained in place through July, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal with 50 to 75 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Gregory and near Pickstown was around 50 percent of normal, with temperatures around 3 degrees above normal during September. Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions eased to moderate (D1) early June, with early month temperatures slightly below normal and rainfall around 50 percent of normal. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Alexandria was less than 25 percent of normal, with temperatures of 4 to 5 degrees above normal during September. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed through the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Menno was around 50 percent of normal, with temperatures around 4 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) at the end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

The storm canceled many school activities in north-central South Dakota. In eastern South Dakota, snowfall amounts at or below one inch combined with high winds to produce blizzard conditions, especially in the open country areas. The blizzard significantly impacted travel - The Watertown Police Department recommended no travel advised within the city limits. There were additional reports of people pulled over in Clark County on Highway 212 and Interstate 29 between Watertown and South Shore due to zero visibility. Numerous school activities were canceled, and businesses closed early due to the horrific weather conditions. Some area schools were delayed or closed on the 14th.

A powerful low pressure system brought damaging high winds, blizzard conditions, and areas of blowing dust from April 12th to April 14th. The combination of heavy snowfall and high winds produced blizzard conditions in Corson and Dewey counties in north-central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6.7 inches in McLaughlin, to 9 inches 14 miles NNE from Isabel, to 14 inches in Keldron. The snowfall and sustained winds of 45 mph with gusts exceeding 55 mph produce over six feet high snowdrifts. The blizzard caused the death of newborn livestock in north-central South Dakota. Mail was delayed in portions of north-central South Dakota as trucks could not leave Bismarck due to dangerous road conditions. The storm canceled many school activities in north-central South Dakota. There were additional reports of people pulled over in Clark County on Highway 212 and Interstate 29 between Watertown and South Shore due to zero visibility. Numerous school activities were canceled, and businesses closed early due to the horrific weather conditions. Some area schools were delayed or closed on the 14th.

A powerful low pressure system produced several severe thunderstorms, high winds, a brief tornado, and freezing rain. The severe storms developed over south-central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 22nd and advanced northward along with a warm front into north-central and northeastern South Dakota into the early morning hours of the 23rd, bringing widespread damage to the area. The storms produced hail, up to two inches in diameter, 60 to 75 mph wind, and locally heavy rainfall. Strong thunderstorm winds rolled a camper on Highway 45 near Sunshine Bible Academy in Hand County. In addition, half-dollar size hail broke windows on a home seven miles southwest of Ridgeview, and two-inch hail broke vehicle and house windows near Ridgeview. Lightning caused some prairie and hay bale fires around the Highmore area. The center of the low-pressure system passed over Aberdeen on April 23rd at 12:20 AM CST, producing a mean sea level pressure of 983.5 mb. The pressure was just 3.5 mb shy of Aberdeen's 980 mb record for April. Additional severe storms developed during the afternoon hours of the 23rd. The storms produce small hail in Campbell County and a brief tornado in Edmunds County. High winds followed the back side of the surface low pressure during the late evening hours on April 22nd into the afternoon hours on April 23rd. Some of the highest wind reports include 79 mph at Herreid, 75 mph 5 miles east of Danforth, 69 mph at Webster, and 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Freezing rain in north-central South Dakota on the 23rd caused ice to accumulate on power lines. With ice accretion and high winds, many power lines went down, mainly in Corson and Dewey county. The storm system was the third significant storm system of April, which further delayed the agricultural planting season.

A strengthening area of low pressure over Minnesota brought high winds to central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota from the afternoon hours on Tuesday, April 5th, through the evening hours on April 7th. Some of the highest wind gusts include 65 mph 17 miles west of Polo and 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport and 64 mph 13 miles north of Vivian. The long-duration wind event caused sporadic tree damage and shingles to blow off of roofs. The Redfield Press reported roof damage to the Northwestern High School in Spink County with different materials, including metal, blown off the school. Light, accumulating snowfall combined with the very strong winds to produce periods of reduced visibility over northeastern South Dakota. Various activities were canceled or postponed, including area schools.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Howard and near Winfred was less than 25 percent of normal, with temperatures of 3 to 4 degrees above normal during September. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed at the very end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Routed water from heavier early-spring precipitation well to the north brought sections of the James River above flood stage for the first time in nearly two years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As water from early spring rains and late winter snowmelt in North Dakota and far northern South Dakota routed downstream, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) climbed into minor flood stage on May 30, and reached 0.28 feet above flood stage at the end of the month. Impacts were limited to inundation of some areas of agricultural land near the river. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High flow continued as a result of heavier May rainfall in upper portions of the basin, with a mid-month crest in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding was ongoing at the start of June 2022 on the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2), with a crest at 2.16 feet above flood stage on June 13. The river fell back to minor flood stage on June 15. A significant amount of rural land was flooded. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall culminated with 1 to 2 inches of rainfall in a small basin near Canton on June 12-13.  Brief minor flooding resulted from the rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rainfall pushed Beaver Creek at Canton (gage site BCCS2) above minor flood stage for a brief time on June 13, with a crest at 0.31 feet above flood stage. A minor amount of agricultural land was flooded.

The prolonged period of high winds caused sporadic tree and shingle damage to roofs. In addition, the combination of ongoing drought conditions and the high winds caused blowing dust in south-central South Dakota. The Hand County Sheriff reported significantly reduced visibility due to blowing dust on the 14th. Some of the highest wind gusts include 73 mph 11 miles south of Bullhead, 72 mph in Tunerville, 71 mph 6 SSW of Peever, and 67 mph 2 miles north of the Pierre Airport and 10 miles SW of Long Lake.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 73 mph was measured by South Dakota State University mesonet site VGAS2 1 mile south of Volga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Canton was around 50 percent of normal, with temperatures from 2 to 3 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cool and wet period at the start of the month eased conditions from severe (D2) drought to moderate (D1) drought early in May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed in early August, but was quickly abated by rainfall from 2 to 4 inches around August 7. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures with precipitation from 50 to 90 percent of normal, led to the development of severe drought conditions in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures of around 2 degrees above normal with 50 to 75 percent of normal precipitation led to the development of severe (D2) drought conditions by late July. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022, with a slow fall in river levels. However, heavy rainfall which totaled 5 to 7 inches fell in a very small portion of the mid to upper-James River Basin during the first week of July and resulted in a mid- to late-month crest before another slow fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022 on the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2). Five to seven inches of rainfall the first seven days of July in a small area at and north of Huron resulted in a crest at 1.21 feet above flood stage on July 21. A significant amount of rural land was flooded. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

A strengthening area of low pressure over Minnesota brought high winds to central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota from the afternoon hours on Tuesday, April 5th, through the evening hours on April 7th. Some of the highest wind gusts include 65 mph 17 miles west of Polo and 2 miles north of the Pierre Regional Airport and 64 mph 13 miles north of Vivian. The long-duration wind event caused sporadic tree damage and shingles to blow off of roofs. The Redfield Press reported roof damage to the Northwestern High School in Spink County with different materials, including metal, blown off the school. Light, accumulating snowfall combined with the very strong winds to produce periods of reduced visibility over northeastern South Dakota. Various activities were canceled or postponed, including area schools.

Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought by the end of June, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through August. Temperatures were around 2 to 3 degrees above normal, with precipitation generally 60 to 75 percent of normal, but locally up to 50 percent above normal near the Missouri River. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Vermillion and near Centerville was around 50 percent of normal, with temperatures around 4 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) during the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022, with a slow fall in river levels. However, heavy rainfall which totaled 5 to 7 inches fell in a very small portion of the mid to upper-James River Basin during the first week of July and resulted in a mid- to late-month crest before another slow fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022 on the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2). Five to seven inches of rainfall the first seven days of July in a small area at and north of Huron resulted in a crest at 1.20 feet above flood stage on July 24. A significant amount of rural land was flooded. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry weather resulted in a gradual drop in water levels along the James River through the month, and flooding ended during early- to mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of August 2022, with the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) at 0.45 feet above flood stage on August 1. A significant amount of rural land was flooded until the river dropped below flood stage on August 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The James River near Columbia rose above the 13-foot flood stage during the morning hours of April 28th. The river reached 15.01 feet, with flooding continuing into May. The river impacted agricultural lands.
Water held back from the Elm Lake Spillway was released, causing minor flooding along the Elm River near Frederick. The flooding impacted agricultural lands along the river.

Water held back from the Elm Lake Spillway was released, causing minor flooding along the Elm River near Frederick. The flooding, which continued into May, impacted agricultural lands along the river.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.

Severe/D2 drought remained unchanged through the first half of April. According to the April 21st release of the U.S. Drought Monitor, there was some improvement over Corson County but worsening across the bootheel of Lyman County. Extreme/D3 drought conditions were the first time D3 was in the Aberdeen forecast area since September 28th, 2021. While beneficial rains occurred, rainfall missed central South Dakota, and precipitation deficits remained high, or 3 to 5 inches over the past six months. The Bad River near Fort Pierre had 7-day average streamflow at the 5th percentile, or much below average. The lack of rainfall continued to cause stock ponds to remain dry or have low water with bad quality across central South Dakota. On the April 26th release of the U.S. Drought Monitor, Corson County was removed from Severe D2 Drought due to heavy wet snowfall on the 14-16 and additional significant precipitation on the 23-24th.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With elevated CAPE of 2500-3000 J/kg and minimal wind shear, rapidly developing updrafts resulted in periodic large hail and spotty damaging wind as storms moved along the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD527 4 miles east-southeast of Forestburg. A peak wind gust of 81 mph occurred at 1338CST. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorm winds demolished half of a cattle shed with debris strewn across the property.  A wooden shed had two sliding doors ripped off, with a large pine tree also uprooted. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through June, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures with below-normal precipitation led to the stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought in early July, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal with 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe/D2 drought remained unchanged through the first half of April. According to the April 21st release of the U.S. Drought Monitor, there was some improvement over Corson County but worsening across the bootheel of Lyman County. Extreme/D3 drought conditions were the first time D3 was in the Aberdeen forecast area since September 28th, 2021. While beneficial rains occurred, rainfall missed central South Dakota, and precipitation deficits remained high, or 3 to 5 inches over the past six months. The Bad River near Fort Pierre had 7-day average streamflow at the 5th percentile, or much below average. The lack of rainfall continued to cause stock ponds to remain dry or have low water with bad quality across central South Dakota. On the April 26th release of the U.S. Drought Monitor, Corson County was removed from Severe D2 Drought due to heavy wet snowfall on the 14-16 and additional significant precipitation on the 23-24th.

A powerful low pressure system produced several severe thunderstorms, high winds, a brief tornado, and freezing rain. The severe storms developed over south-central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 22nd and advanced northward along with a warm front into north-central and northeastern South Dakota into the early morning hours of the 23rd, bringing widespread damage to the area. The storms produced hail, up to two inches in diameter, 60 to 75 mph wind, and locally heavy rainfall. Strong thunderstorm winds rolled a camper on Highway 45 near Sunshine Bible Academy in Hand County. In addition, half-dollar size hail broke windows on a home seven miles southwest of Ridgeview, and two-inch hail broke vehicle and house windows near Ridgeview. Lightning caused some prairie and hay bale fires around the Highmore area. The center of the low-pressure system passed over Aberdeen on April 23rd at 12:20 AM CST, producing a mean sea level pressure of 983.5 mb. The pressure was just 3.5 mb shy of Aberdeen's 980 mb record for April. Additional severe storms developed during the afternoon hours of the 23rd. The storms produce small hail in Campbell County and a brief tornado in Edmunds County. High winds followed the back side of the surface low pressure during the late evening hours on April 22nd into the afternoon hours on April 23rd. Some of the highest wind reports include 79 mph at Herreid, 75 mph 5 miles east of Danforth, 69 mph at Webster, and 4 miles southwest of Mound City. Freezing rain in north-central South Dakota on the 23rd caused ice to accumulate on power lines. With ice accretion and high winds, many power lines went down, mainly in Corson and Dewey county. The storm system was the third significant storm system of April, which further delayed the agricultural planting season.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions eased to moderate (D1) early June, with early month temperatures slightly below normal and rainfall around 50 percent of normal. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Mitchell was less than 10 percent of normal, with temperatures of 3 to 4 degrees above normal during September. Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) at mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

Severe/D2 drought across mainly Dewey, Stanley and Sully counties at the beginning of the month expanded across most areas along and west of the Missouri River by the end of the month, due to below normal precipitation through the month by half an inch to an inch. Stock ponds remained dry across central SD, and fire danger was an issue whenever low humidity combined with strong winds due to a lack of snow cover for many.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage from north to south across the area led to strong to locally high winds during the afternoon on the 25th. Northwesterly wind gusts were measured in excess of 60 mph near Herreid, Lees Corner and Summit.

A cold frontal passage from north to south across the area led to strong to locally high winds during the afternoon on the 25th. Northwesterly wind gusts were measured in excess of 60 mph near Herreid, Lees Corner and Summit.

A storm system tracked from the northern Rockies to the central Plains, producing snow from the Black Hills area into south central South Dakota. Precipitation started as some light freezing rain or freezing drizzle in some areas, especially toward south central South Dakota. The highest amounts of snow fell across southwestern South Dakota, where three to around six inches of snow were reported, with mostly two or three inches elsewhere. Brisk winds produced some blowing and especially drifting snow on the plains.

An area of low pressure moved from Nebraska into central Iowa from the night of March 29th through the morning of the 30th. It produced strong to isolated high northerly winds across the area, including a 61 mph gust near Gann Valley. Additionally, a broad area of light to moderate wet snow accumulated 1 to 5 inches across central and northeastern SD, and led to hazardous road conditions and school delays.

The highest amounts of snow fell across southwestern South Dakota, where three to around six inches of snow were reported, with mostly two or three inches elsewhere. Brisk winds produced some blowing and especially drifting snow on the plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of the grandstand at a local speedway was severely damaged by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to have reached 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions eased to moderate (D1) mid-June, with early month temperatures slightly below normal and rainfall around 50 percent of normal. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened slightly to extreme (D3) during early August. Temperatures were two to three degrees above normal, with precipitation generally 50 to 70 percent of normal. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures with below-normal precipitation led to the stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions remained in place through July, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 50 to 75 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail, up to the size of golf balls in Garden City, was associated with a series of supercell storms that formed during the early morning hours of May 9th. Storms also produced winds up to 62 mph. The overall system also helped generate some post-frontal non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 67 mph near Sisseton and 62 mph in Turnerville later that afternoon.
A cold front brought windy conditions as it swept through north central South Dakota on the evening of the 15th. The strongest gust was 58 mph two miles north of Herreid.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was measured at an SDDOT mesonet station two miles north of Herreid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently 40 to 44 mph at Automated Surface Observing System site KHON 2 miles north-northwest of Huron and the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD582 3 miles north-northwest of Wolsey. A peak wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at KHON at 1053CST.

Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 300 yards to the south of the farm a calf shed was picked|up and lofted to the north, over the shelterbelt on the south|side of the property. The calf shed would land in the feed lot|area and pushed up against a barn. A stave silo was moved off the|foundation about a foot, but otherwise remained erect. A pole barn |was severely damaged, with the west wall collapsed and the south wall|pushed in. A wood grain bin shifted off the foundation and was|pushed 300 feet to the north. Two empty grain bins were|destroyed. One tossed into a creek on the north end of the|property. The second was destroyed and lofted. A cattle trailer|at the same location was pushed about 100 feet before it was|rolled into the creek. Pieces of debris from the farm would be|found across Highway 12 and over a mile to the north northeast of|the property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air with highs in the 90s and dewpoints around 70 degrees set up across central and eastern South Dakota during the day Friday. This was just ahead of a front that was slowly migrating southeast, though the frontal passage brought little relief as temperatures on the north side of the front still topped out close to the century mark, with dewpoints remaining in the 60s. Morning lows both Friday and Saturday remained in the 70s for most of the area. A peak heat index value of 120 degrees was observed at weather stations in Mellette and Onaka, with over 70 weather stations in central, north central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index of 100 degrees or higher at some point during the day.

A cluster of storms moved along the Interstate 90 corridor in central South Dakota during the afternoon before pivoting northeast into east central South Dakota during the evening. The system produced a number of reports of winds with an intensity around 60 mph, with a peak gust of 79 mph at Poinsett Colony.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated wind speeds of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The south to north tornado track started south of 156th St along 482nd Ave where a farm exhibited clear tornado damage which included mangled trees, barn roof damage, barn doors split in half, residential roof and siding damage and a camper completely destroyed. The track would continue roughly three miles north in the vicinity of 153rd St. Another farm would sustain damage, with tops of trees missing in one shelterbelt and uprooted and snapped trees in a second shelterbelt. The dormer of the residence was ripped off and landed 85 to 100 yards from the house in a northeast direction. North of this property a dairy farm had a machine shed with collapsed walls. This farm was housing dairy cattle that were transported from the Estelline area after a May 12 tornado destroyed barns on that property. The last indication of the tornado track was through a shelter belt a quarter mile north of 153rd St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.

Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.

While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near average with precipitation slightly below normal until the very end of the month, which led to continuation of severe (D2) drought conditions through May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well-below normal precipitation and above-normal temperatures contributed to steady to worsening drought conditions in areas near the Missouri River, while some areas to the north received periodic rains to help alleviate drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought by the end of June, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal and 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were generally slightly above normal during August. However, precipitation ranged between 25 to 75 percent of normal across most of southeast South Dakota, with very localized amounts from 100 to 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through August. Temperatures were around 2 to 3 degrees above normal, with precipitation generally 60 to 75 percent of normal, but locally up to 50 percent above normal near the Missouri River. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above-normal temperatures with below-normal precipitation led to the stagnation or slight worsening of severe to extreme drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) drought in late July, which featured temperatures of 2 to 3 degrees above normal with 50 to 75 percent of normal precipitation. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Yankton was around 50 percent of normal, with temperatures around 4 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions worsened to exceptional (D4) during the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph was measured by South Dakota State University mesonet site SFSS2 in Sioux Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Routed water from heavier early-spring precipitation well to the north brought sections of the James River above flood stage for the first time in nearly two years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As water from early spring rains and late winter snowmelt in North Dakota and far northern South Dakota routed downstream, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) climbed into minor flood stage on May 30, and reached 0.28 feet above flood stage at the end of the month. Impacts were limited to inundation of some areas of agricultural land near the river. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions developed across portions of southeast South Dakota during mid-April after a winter and early spring of below normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed mid-month with only 25 to 50 percent of normal January through March precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneath a broad stratiform area of precipitation which expanded from earlier and stronger convection across south central South Dakota and central Nebraska, a wake low developed across southeast South Dakota by late evening. In a narrow zone from near Mitchell to north of Pipestone, winds suddenly increased to 40 to 50 mph, with a brief period of gusts from 58 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds 13 miles west of Ward at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD577 reached a sustained 40 to 50 mph, with a gust to 65 mph at 2204CST.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a Big Stone City business was blown off, and parts of it ended up in a tree about 360 feet to the north by estimated 80 mph winds.

One|2x4 pierced through the garage attached to the house. Debris|would continue to be found about a quarter mile to the northeast,|across an open field and into the neighbor's farm yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air with highs in the 90s and dewpoints around 70 degrees set up across central and eastern South Dakota during the day Friday. This was just ahead of a front that was slowly migrating southeast, though the frontal passage brought little relief as temperatures on the north side of the front still topped out close to the century mark, with dewpoints remaining in the 60s. Morning lows both Friday and Saturday remained in the 70s for most of the area. A peak heat index value of 120 degrees was observed at weather stations in Mellette and Onaka, with over 70 weather stations in central, north central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index of 100 degrees or higher at some point during the day.

A cluster of marginally severe storms developed along the Missouri River Valley in central South Dakota during the heat of the afternoon. Peak winds associated with these storms was measured at 60 mph in Oacoma.

River flooding from snowmelt and rainfall earlier this spring continued into August, though rivers would see a slow decline due to drier conditions. The James River at Ashton fell below flood stage on August 15th at 18:15 CST, while the gauges at Stratford and Columbia remained in flood stage the entire month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in south central South Dakota the last half of the night, and moved northeast along the nose of a strong low-level jet toward east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota after sunrise.  Elevated instability of 1000-2000J/kg and deep-layer elevated shear of 30-40 knots led to organization of storms into multicell structures capable of spotty large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 60 mph was measured by a personal weather station.

A couple rounds of thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of an elongated area of low pressure during the evening of the 19th across central South Dakota, and then tracked north-northeast. Straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were produced, including at three miles south of Bunker (70 mph), and five miles west-southwest of Mound City (70 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was measured by a personal weather station. A steel tank was tipped over at a farm a mile west of Mound City as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553 4 miles west of Plankinton were from 40 to 45 mph, with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at 1445CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently from 40 to 44 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System site KMHE 3 miles north of Mitchell, with a peak gust of 60 mph at 1620CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was measured at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD570 1 mile northwest of Tripp at 1345CST.  Sustained winds were periodically from 40 to 45 mph, as well as at site SD530 5 miles south of Freeman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD588 1 mile southwest of Pickstown were mainly from 40 to 54 mph, with a peak gust of 69 mph at 0234CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grain bin in a field near 496th Ave and 179th Street was ripped from the base and tossed a mile to the northeast. Roughly three and a half miles northeast on 176th St a farmstead lost several outbuildings and had roof and window damage to the home. Another two miles to the northeast three metal truss transmission towers were damaged, one completely twisted and collapsed. The strongest winds were confined to the area of the transmission towers. Roughly three and a half miles northeast, just north of US Highway 212, a shed was destroyed and a path was noted through a shelterbelt. The very last indications of the tornado was north of 169th Street on 476th Ave where the path was noted through a shelter belt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 73 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD504 4 miles south of Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a peak wind gust of 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD605 3 miles east-southeast of Parker measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter four miles north of Cresbard estimated 60 mph wind gusts, which blew a few shingles off of his roof.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.

Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two properties near the intersection of 164th St and 475th Ave sustained damage. The first farm had a roof ripped off a barn, damage to a machine shed, a destroyed stave silo, debris pierced through house walls, roof panels off house, broken house windows, snapped and uprooted trees, and a dented grain bin. The second residence had half a barn roof ripped off and tossed a quarter mile to the northeast. The track continued northeast roughly six miles, crossing Grant County Highway 15, between 158th St and 159th St. Near this location a farmstead sustained complete loss of a machine shed and power lines were downed. The track would come to an end roughly two more miles to the northeast near 157th St, where another farm would sustain complete loss to a machine shed and debris spread across the field to the north northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 2 to 3 inches from May 8 to May 12 across northeast into east central portions of South Dakota resulted in a brief period of minor flooding along the upper portions of the Big Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As runoff from rainfall of 2 to 3 inches across northeast South Dakota was routed downstream, the Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) climbed into minor flood stage on May 13, and crested 0.24 feet above flood stage on May 14. The river fell below flood stage on May 15. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 2 to 3 inches from May 8 to May 12 across northeast into east central portions of South Dakota resulted in a brief period of minor flooding along the upper portions of the Big Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As runoff from rainfall of 2 to 3 inches across northeast South Dakota was routed downstream, the Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) climbed into minor flood stage and crested 0.43 feet above flood stage on May 13. The river fell below flood stage on May 14. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 2 to 3 inches from May 8 to May 12 across northeast into east central portions of South Dakota resulted in a brief period of minor flooding along the upper portions of the Big Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred in the basin north of Pipestone on May 11, and resultant runoff produced a quick rise into minor flood stage. Pipestone Creek below Pipestone (gage site PSTM5) crested briefly 0.09 feet above flood stage early on May 12. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 80 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One mile south of Gary, SD a farm sustained significant tornado damage. The home on the property lost the entire roof, along with most of the external walls on the main floor of the house. Only a portion of the internal walls remained standing. The resident home|at the time was able to make it to the basement, however did sustain minor injuries when a refrigerator from the main floor fell down through the ceiling. The attached garage was completely blown off the foundation, and the pickup in the garage was pushed at least 6 feet from where it was originally parked. Nearby outbuildings sustained roof damage, including a hole in one roof that was likely caused from flying debris. The tornado continued to track north northeast of the farm into an open corn stubble field. Rotation was noted in the stubble. The debris track was spread across the field with large pieces of wood and other construction materials pierced vertically into the ground. Further to the north northeast a semi was turned 180 degrees and flipped|on its side. The debris path continued across 180th Street and was last noted in a tree grove approximately 0.3 miles from the residence. The resident also noted that the family dog was seen being swept out of the house during the tornado, but was later |found safe with only minor injuries after walking to a neighbor's farm about one mile away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in the Missouri River Valley in the afternoon producing quarter to half dollar sized hail. These storms merged into a line which would eventually produce three weak tornadoes. One of these tornadoes destroyed a shed near Columbia. The system moved east of the James River Valley in the evening with limited severe weather thereafter. Additionally, heavy rain caused flooding in Edmunds county which had already seen minor water issues due to persistent early May rainfall. Some strong to high non-thunderstorm gusts also occurred with this system, including 66 mph in Agar and 57 mph in both the Polo and Summit areas.

The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred 4 miles north of Lake Kampeska. The tornado|hit the northeast side of a house and removed the the garage from|the house, along with part of the roof. The garage was tossed to|the north.  An outbuilding to the north of the house was also|destroyed and thrown to the north and northwest. The debris was|scattered at least a half mile north and northwest of the|location of the house. The family sought shelter in the basement|of the home and they were safe.

Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A clear path was observed through a tree grove. Additionally, farm machinery was tossed around on the property.

Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.

A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Canton and near Centerville was around 50 percent of normal, with temperatures from 2 to 3 degrees above normal during September. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were near average with precipitation slightly below normal until the very end of the month, which led to continuation of severe (D2) drought conditions through May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were brought down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few branches were downed by severe winds.
