EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large door was blown in, and the metal roof peeled off an outbuilding.

A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.

Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.

An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.

A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were from 40 to 49 mph, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph at 2115CST at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553 4 miles west of Plankinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station 2 miles west-northwest of Scotland measured occasional sustained winds from 40 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven power poles were broken or tipped over along a 2/3 mile stretch of Highway 212. All of the poles were in a swampy area or in water. The damage was just south of a recreation area that also saw a few trees uprooted and a camper tipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.

A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel hail was observed in addition to the severe wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 107 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD570 2 miles northwest of Tripp.  The town grain elevated was destroyed.

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms produced gusty winds across portions of Todd County. Monsoonal moisture, combined with training storms, resulted in flash flooding across southern Mellette and northern Todd Counties. Rainfall amounts of three to four inches were reported. Flash flooding quickly occurred as water ponded in fields. Heavy rain washed gravel off rural roads. The Little White River around the confluence with Cut Meat Creek and Oak Creek rose several feet in an hour.

Flash flooding quickly occurred as water ponded in fields. Heavy rain washed gravel off rural roads. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain quickly flooded fields and washed gravel off rural roads. The Little White River around the confluence with Cut Meat Creek and Oak Creek rose several feet in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were from 40 to 45 mph, with a peak wind gust of 59 mph at 2102CST at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD570 1 mile northwest of Tripp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated wind speeds of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The James River continued to flood from May into and through June near Columbia, Stratford and Ashton, due to heavy rainfall. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded along with a few area roads, as river gauges reached moderate to major flood categories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Ashton began the month in major flood stage, and crested at 17.51 feet at 10:30am on June 9th. It dropped into moderate flood stage during the early morning hours of June 21st, and remained there through the rest of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of light to moderate rain on top of saturated soils resulted in brief minor flooding for multiple rivers in the forecast area. Rainfall amounts for the 5 day period leading up to the flooding totaled just less than 3 inches near these river basins. Flooding developed on May 12th in the afternoon, with Mud Creek near Rauville peaking just at the flood stage of 9.0 feet before falling below flood stage on the morning of May 13th. Oak Creek near Wakpala reached a stage of 10.68 feet briefly before falling back below flood stage later that evening. The Big Sioux River near Castlewood also topped out at the flood stage of 9.0 feet and stayed at that level until the early morning hours of May 16th. No impacts were noted.

There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening storms developed across western and south central South Dakota before expanding north and east through the course of the night, either as individual cells or as small clusters. Hail and wind damage was isolated but spread across the area. The bowing segment of a thunderstorm produced an estimated 70 mph wind gust west of Trail City, which knocked down a Cottonwood tree. One of the stronger clusters also generated hail up to the size of golf balls north of Oacoma, while another cluster moved into far eastern South Dakota and produce hail to half dollar size in Lake Poinsett later that morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire department employee estimated 70 mph wind gusts, which downed a nearby large cottonwood tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph occurred at 1648CST at Automated Surface Observing System site KFSD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneath a broad stratiform area of precipitation which expanded from earlier and stronger convection across south central South Dakota and central Nebraska, a wake low developed across southeast South Dakota by late evening. In a narrow zone from near Mitchell to north of Pipestone, winds suddenly increased to 40 to 50 mph, with a brief period of gusts from 58 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds 3 miles north of Mitchell at Automated Surface Observing System site KMHE reached a sustained 40 to 45 mph, with a gust to 62 mph at 2104CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The residents were outside when they saw the tornado quickly develop and were forced to seek shelter under a tractor. The tornado ripped the entire roof off of a newly built machine shed and also damaged the exterior walls. Debris was spread to the north, with sheet metal and pieces of rafters lofted into nearby trees.  The tornado continued to track north northwest about a half mile. The tops of trees were snapped off in one shelterbelt and a clear path was evident in a second shelterbelt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in the Missouri River Valley in the afternoon producing quarter to half dollar sized hail. These storms merged into a line which would eventually produce three weak tornadoes. One of these tornadoes destroyed a shed near Columbia. The system moved east of the James River Valley in the evening with limited severe weather thereafter. Additionally, heavy rain caused flooding in Edmunds county which had already seen minor water issues due to persistent early May rainfall. Some strong to high non-thunderstorm gusts also occurred with this system, including 66 mph in Agar and 57 mph in both the Polo and Summit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A security camera captured a brief tornado touchdown in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.

Flash flooding quickly occurred as water ponded in fields. Heavy rain washed gravel off rural roads. The Little White River around the confluence with Cut Meat Creek and Oak Creek rose several feet in an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain quickly flooded fields and washed gravel off rural roads. The Little White River rose a couple of feet in an hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts around 60 mph.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over the southern Black Hills and tracked southeast, weakening on the adjacent plains. The storm produced large hail and heavy rain over the area. Other strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flash flooding over far southwestern South Dakota. Rain amounts of three to four inches were estimated in some areas. In Oglala Lakota County, flash flooding quickly occurred in Porcupine, with water running over roads. In Custer County, Hopkins Flat Road west and northwest of Pringle was washed out.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage occurred along Van Metre road between 234th St and 237th St. This included 3 farms which sustained significant damage. The first farm lost an entire section of roof off an open gable style roof on the primary residence, hay bales were toppled over, a trailer rotated and tipped to the east, a bulk feed bin, along with other debris, was tossed and spread over a half mile east. The next farm also lost panels off the cross gable style roof, dented grain bins, a bent flag pole, a combine header moved 100 yards to the east, and other farm equipment displaced. A deck was completely destroyed. Debris from the farm was spread at least a quarter mile east. The third farm had a feeder wagon flipped, a machine shed completely destroyed, a second outbuilding destroyed and two empty grain bins ripped from the foundation. Debris from the property was spread at least a quarter mile to the east. The damage across these three farms was extensive and was caused by intense and persistent straight line winds. Eyewitness accounts and radar data suggest severe winds stronger than 60 mph lasted for 15 minutes straight with no reprieve. At the peak, winds are estimated to range from 115 to 130 mph. This is concurrent with major loss of roof panels on both homes; both of which exhibited strong roof construction.

Some strong to high non-thunderstorm gusts also occurred with this system, including 66 mph in Agar and 57 mph in both the Polo and Summit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was reported by an emergency manager.

An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The track started near 239th street, about 0.75 miles east of 264th Ave. The path was clearly noted through a shelter belt. The tornado continued another half mile where it damaged a farm. The primary residence had a quarter of the roof panels ripped off a gable style roof and multiple windows and glass doors blown out. Two outbuildings were completely destroyed. Another shed lost the entire tin roof. The debris track from the farm continued for about a quarter of a mile northeast. The resident���s pickup truck also noted damage including the back window broken and dents from flying debris. The tornado weakened, but continued roughly 3.5 miles northeast to Van Metre Rd where it damaged another farm.  At this property a farmhouse had shingle damage to only half of the roof. A well-built machine shed was missing about a quarter of the roof panels. A grain cart was also flipped to the north.

A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball size hail smashed county deputy sheriff's windshield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon and evening of April 22, 2022 and moved northward with an advancing surface warm front. A couple thunderstorms took on supercell characteristics near the surface boundary and produced large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter. A brief tornado also occurred near Wolsey, SD.||In the post warm frontal air mass from mid-evening into the early overnight of April 23, 2022, non-thunderstorm high winds produced multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping-pong ball sized hail dropped just west of Bonilla and U.S. Highway 281. Power was knocked out in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14.  Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with seven overturned semi-trucks across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently from 40 to 45 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KMDS in Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail, up to the size of golf balls in Garden City, was associated with a series of supercell storms that formed during the early morning hours of May 9th. Storms also produced winds up to 62 mph. The overall system also helped generate some post-frontal non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 67 mph near Sisseton and 62 mph in Turnerville later that afternoon.

A strong low pressure system tracked from western North Dakota into the southwestern Canadian Province of Manitoba during the morning hours of May 13th. Strong to high non-thunderstorm winds resulted during this time.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over the southern Black Hills and tracked southeast, weakening on the adjacent plains. The storm produced large hail and heavy rain over the area. Other strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flash flooding over far southwestern South Dakota. Rain amounts of three to four inches were estimated in some areas. In Oglala Lakota County, flash flooding quickly occurred in Porcupine, with water running over roads. In Custer County, Hopkins Flat Road west and northwest of Pringle was washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain washed out Hopkins Flat Road west and northwest of Pringle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado 7 miles to the south-southeast of Webster hit an|outbuilding, pulling the roof off and knocking over the interior|wall. The debris from the building was carried to the northwest|for over 250 yards and spread out to the west. A calving shed was|rolled to the north and then northwest. A animal trailer was|tipped over and a metal windmill was knocked over and twisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 50 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD532 near Gayville, with a peak wind gust of 66 mph at 0945CST.  Automated Weather Observing System site KYKN measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph at 0928CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch was downed by estimated 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph occurred at 1435CST at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD520 2 miles west of Dell Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones up to 2.0 inches in diameter.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in south central South Dakota the last half of the night, and moved northeast along the nose of a strong low-level jet toward east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota after sunrise.  Elevated instability of 1000-2000J/kg and deep-layer elevated shear of 30-40 knots led to organization of storms into multicell structures capable of spotty large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured frequent wind gusts to 60 mph between 0310-0320CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few shingles were removed from a home by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was recorded by a personal weather station.

Evening storms developed across western and south central South Dakota before expanding north and east through the course of the night, either as individual cells or as small clusters. Hail and wind damage was isolated but spread across the area. The bowing segment of a thunderstorm produced an estimated 70 mph wind gust west of Trail City, which knocked down a Cottonwood tree. One of the stronger clusters also generated hail up to the size of golf balls north of Oacoma, while another cluster moved into far eastern South Dakota and produce hail to half dollar size in Lake Poinsett later that morning.

The James River continued to flood from May into and through June near Columbia, Stratford and Ashton, due to heavy rainfall. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded along with a few area roads, as river gauges reached moderate to major flood categories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Columbia began the month in Major flood stage, and was at its highest at midnight on June 1st at 18.41 feet. The gauge dropped below 18 feet into Moderate flood stage during the late morning on June 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in association with a low pressure system and stationary front across western South Dakota late on June 11th, and then spread east-southeast to affect portions of south central South Dakota during the early morning on June 12th. Two different rounds of thunderstorms produced a 58 mph wind gust 9 miles NNW of Vivian, and quarter sized hail near Westover.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD520 3 miles west of Dell Rapids.

Five healthy spruce trees were uprooted and several deciduous trees snapped at another farm, where the roof was also lost off a horse shelter. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

The overall system also helped generate some post-frontal non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 67 mph near Sisseton and 62 mph in Turnerville later that afternoon.

A strong low pressure system tracked from western North Dakota into the southwestern Canadian Province of Manitoba during the morning hours of May 13th. Strong to high non-thunderstorm winds resulted during this time.

Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD605 4 miles east-southeast of Parker were occasionally from 40 to 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14.  Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with seven overturned semi-trucks across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently from 40 to 44 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KBKX in Brookings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Routed water from heavier early-spring precipitation well to the north brought sections of the James River above flood stage for the first time in nearly two years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As water from early spring rains and late winter snowmelt in North Dakota and far northern South Dakota routed downstream, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) climbed into minor flood stage on May 30, and reached 0.28 feet above flood stage at the end of the month. Impacts were limited to inundation of some areas of agricultural land near the river. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms became severe as they moved east of the Black Hills across the west central South Dakota plains. They formed into a line as they moved toward central South Dakota, with wind gusts over 60 mph observed across parts of the area.

Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe winds resulted in lots of tree branch damage. Small hail was observed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 47 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites SD547 near Montrose, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph at 1030CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted in Ortley by estimated 75 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon and evening of April 22, 2022 and moved northward with an advancing surface warm front. A couple thunderstorms took on supercell characteristics near the surface boundary and produced large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter. A brief tornado also occurred near Wolsey, SD.||In the post warm frontal air mass from mid-evening into the early overnight of April 23, 2022, non-thunderstorm high winds produced multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts removed the roof from a shed around 7 miles south of Marion.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first indications of tornado damage were just east of Interstate 29 near 186th Street. A farm had mangled tree branches through the tops of the shelter belt, a destroyed outbuilding, a west facing garage door and garage rafters bowed in and three pickups with the back windows broken out. The track continued a quarter mile to the north, where a second farm had uprooted trees, complete destruction of a machine shed and a collapsed barn. The main residence also had loss of shingles and the loss of a small portion of roof panels. The track continued northeast. The next residence to sustain damage was near 772nd Ave and 181st St. The tornado entered the farm from the west where it uprooted several trees and broke branches off the tops of others, a third of a roughly 300 foot long milk barn was leveled, one stave silo completely collapsed and a second was razed about half way to the base. Further east on the property a shelter belt was managed and an empty grain bin was tossed east northeast about a quarter mile into the neighboring field. An outbuilding south of the property also collapsed. Just shy of a mile to the northeast along 473rd Ave a machine shed buckled on the southwest side. Another quarter mile to the northeast another farm sustained significant damage. On this farm a tornado path was noted through the trees on the west side of house. Trees were uprooted and one tree twisted counter clockwise. On the main residence eight sheets of plywood were ripped off the roof and insulation was blown out of the attic. Roof material was found in trees and debris spread 200 or 300 yards to the north northeast. Residents were in the basement at the time and noticed that their ears popped. They also noted that water was drained from toilets and plumbing traps after the storm. Power lines were downed near 474th Ave. Another several miles to the northeast, near 179th Street and 475th Ave, another farm sustained significant damage. On the south side of the property several pieces of farm equipment were moved, tipped or tossed and a grain bin was dented. Trees were uprooted south of the house and the house sustained window damage. Walls collapsed on both a barn and machine shed on the property. Another smaller shed was also destroyed. A path of debris was noted about a quarter mile northeast of the property. The last property damaged by the tornado was located at 177th Street and US Highway 15. This property had tree damage and lost a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down by estimated 70 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was measured by  South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD550 3 miles southeast of Jefferson.

A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.

A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe. A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

Severe/D2 drought conditions persisted through May with Pierre at 2 inches below normal for the month. Some areas on the periphery of the drought area did see improvement with near normal moisture in May, as was the case in Mobridge, but conditions remained unchanged for the most part across the core area of drought which includes Dewey, Stanley and Jones counties.

The Big Sioux River was in minor flood stage near Castlewood from the end of May through June 4th, and in minor flood stage at Broadway Avenue in Watertown from the end of May through the morning of June 1st. The flooding was a result of prior heavy rainfall, and resulted in minor impacts to low-lying agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Broadway Avenue in Watertown was in minor flood stage from the end of May through the morning of June 1st. It crested at 10.62 feet at midnight on June 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in association with a low pressure system and stationary front across western South Dakota late on June 11th, and then spread east-southeast to affect portions of south central South Dakota during the early morning on June 12th. Two different rounds of thunderstorms produced a 58 mph wind gust 9 miles NNW of Vivian, and quarter sized hail near Westover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged between the size of peas and quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of an elongated area of low pressure during the evening of the 19th across central South Dakota, and then tracked north-northeast. Straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were produced, including at three miles south of Bunker (70 mph), and five miles west-southwest of Mound City (70 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve mature pine trees were snapped by thunderstorm wind gusts at the Moe Lutheran Parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD577 13 miles west of Ward were mainly from 40 to 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi tractor trailer was blown over on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14.  Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with seven overturned semi-trucks across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently from 40 to 48 mph, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 1304CST at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD605 4 miles east-southeast of Parker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 48 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites SD518 near Cavour and SD582 near Wolsey, with peak winds gusts of 62 mph (1230CST) and 61 mph (1220CST) respectively.  A peak wind gust of 69 mph was recorded at Automated Surface Observing System site KHON near Huron at 1146CST.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch tree limbs were broken and shingles were blown off a roof by estimated 80 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones up to 2.0 inches in diameter.

Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones up to 2.0 inches in diameter.

Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elm tree was snapped, and several 2 to 4 inch diameter branches were downed by estimated 70 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.

Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.

Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust was recorded at 8:44pm CST at the Watertown airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly spun up 6 miles north-northwest of Montrose in an open field. The tornado caused no damage from which the NWS could assign an EF-scale rating.  The average path width was estimated at 25 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional quarter to ping-pong ball size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain generated flooding along the Little Minnesota River near Peever. Flood stage was exceeded on May 10 at 17.69 feet but fell below flood stage on May 11 in the early morning hours. Impacts from this flood event are unknown.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable roof damage and numerous trees with broken or snapped limbs across town due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe/D2 drought conditions persisted through May with Pierre at 2 inches below normal for the month. Some areas on the periphery of the drought area did see improvement with near normal moisture in May, as was the case in Mobridge, but conditions remained unchanged for the most part across the core area of drought which includes Dewey, Stanley and Jones counties.

Evening storms developed across western and south central South Dakota before expanding north and east through the course of the night, either as individual cells or as small clusters. Hail and wind damage was isolated but spread across the area. The bowing segment of a thunderstorm produced an estimated 70 mph wind gust west of Trail City, which knocked down a Cottonwood tree. One of the stronger clusters also generated hail up to the size of golf balls north of Oacoma, while another cluster moved into far eastern South Dakota and produce hail to half dollar size in Lake Poinsett later that morning.

Multiple days of light to moderate rain on top of saturated soils resulted in brief minor flooding for multiple rivers in the forecast area. Rainfall amounts for the 5 day period leading up to the flooding totaled just less than 3 inches near these river basins. Flooding developed on May 12th in the afternoon, with Mud Creek near Rauville peaking just at the flood stage of 9.0 feet before falling below flood stage on the morning of May 13th. Oak Creek near Wakpala reached a stage of 10.68 feet briefly before falling back below flood stage later that evening. The Big Sioux River near Castlewood also topped out at the flood stage of 9.0 feet and stayed at that level until the early morning hours of May 16th. No impacts were noted.

Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Another cold front-induced line of convection developed later in the evening across eastern Nebraska and northwest Iowa, with the primary threat being damaging winds. ||More widespread synoptic high winds followed the passage of a strong cold front. Peak wind gusts in excess of 58 mph occurred across much of southeast South Dakota and into portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported golf ball size hail and a 50 mph wind gust.
