Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14.  Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with seven overturned semi-trucks across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently from 40 to 47 mph, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD582 3 miles north-northwest of Wolsey at 0327CST.  Strong wind gusts were also measured at 60 mph 2 miles north-northwest of Huron at 0814CST and 62 mph 3 miles east of Cavour at 0637CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph at times during the afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD504 near Arlington, with a peak wind gust of 64 mph at 1521CST.

Storms formed in the Missouri River Valley in the afternoon producing quarter to half dollar sized hail. These storms merged into a line which would eventually produce three weak tornadoes. One of these tornadoes destroyed a shed near Columbia. The system moved east of the James River Valley in the evening with limited severe weather thereafter. Additionally, heavy rain caused flooding in Edmunds county which had already seen minor water issues due to persistent early May rainfall. Some strong to high non-thunderstorm gusts also occurred with this system, including 66 mph in Agar and 57 mph in both the Polo and Summit areas.

An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was measured at the SDDOT RWIS station five miles south-southwest of Miranda at 12:56am CST.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.

There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

The portion of the James River at Stratford began to reach flood stage during the early morning on May 7th, and crested at 18.09 feet by the end of May. The main impact was flooding of agricultural lands.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD530 5 miles south of Freeman. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe/D2 drought conditions persisted through May with Pierre at 2 inches below normal for the month. Some areas on the periphery of the drought area did see improvement with near normal moisture in May, as was the case in Mobridge, but conditions remained unchanged for the most part across the core area of drought which includes Dewey, Stanley and Jones counties.

Some areas on the periphery of the drought area did see improvement with near normal moisture in May, as was the case in Mobridge, but conditions remained unchanged for the most part across the core area of drought which includes Dewey, Stanley and Jones counties.

Excessive rain in the James basin resulted in the river at Ashton entering flood stage on May 20th, and at the end of the month the peak stage was 16.56 feet. At this level, impacts are primarily limited to flooded agricultural lands.

Multiple days of light to moderate rain on top of saturated soils resulted in brief minor flooding for multiple rivers in the forecast area. Rainfall amounts for the 5 day period leading up to the flooding totaled just less than 3 inches near these river basins. Flooding developed on May 12th in the afternoon, with Mud Creek near Rauville peaking just at the flood stage of 9.0 feet before falling below flood stage on the morning of May 13th. Oak Creek near Wakpala reached a stage of 10.68 feet briefly before falling back below flood stage later that evening. The Big Sioux River near Castlewood also topped out at the flood stage of 9.0 feet and stayed at that level until the early morning hours of May 16th. No impacts were noted.

Evening storms developed across western and south central South Dakota before expanding north and east through the course of the night, either as individual cells or as small clusters. Hail and wind damage was isolated but spread across the area. The bowing segment of a thunderstorm produced an estimated 70 mph wind gust west of Trail City, which knocked down a Cottonwood tree. One of the stronger clusters also generated hail up to the size of golf balls north of Oacoma, while another cluster moved into far eastern South Dakota and produce hail to half dollar size in Lake Poinsett later that morning.

A weak low pressure system triggered several thunderstorms in a hot and favorable environment across north central South Dakota on the evening of the 23rd. The strongest of these storms produced large hail, ranging from nickel to two inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel sized hail was reported, with very little wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms came up through south central South Dakota in the evening and overnight hours, and proceeded northeast across central and eastern South Dakota with multiple reports of 1 inch hail and wind gusts of 60 mph.

Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust was 63 mph, measured at 9:18pm CST at the SDDOT mesonet station four miles west of Bryant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall persisted across the area between April 28th and May 1st with three day totals over 3 inches between Edmunds, McPherson and Brown counties as well as upstream in North Dakota. This, combined with planned releases due to construction on the Elm Lake Spillway, resulted in minor flooding along the Elm River. Flooding was already occurring during the morning of May 1st and continued into May 2nd with a crest of 13.95 feet at the gauge near Frederick. The gauge at Westport went into flood stage on May 2nd, peaked in stage at 14.4 feet around mid day on May 4th, and then rapidly fell below flood stage on the morning of May 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elm River gauge near Frederick peaked at 13.95 feet on the afternoon of May 1st and receded below flood stage in the afternoon of May 2nd. Agricultural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 44 mph at times during the afternoon at Automated Weather Observing System site KBKX at Brookings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14.  Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with seven overturned semi-trucks across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently from 40 to 44 mph, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph measured at 0813CST by the Automated Surface Observing System site KMHE 3 miles north-northwest of Mitchell.

A brief tornado also occurred near Wolsey, SD.||In the post warm frontal air mass from mid-evening into the early overnight of April 23, 2022, non-thunderstorm high winds produced multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph across southeast South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in the Missouri River Valley in the afternoon producing quarter to half dollar sized hail. These storms merged into a line which would eventually produce three weak tornadoes. One of these tornadoes destroyed a shed near Columbia. The system moved east of the James River Valley in the evening with limited severe weather thereafter. Additionally, heavy rain caused flooding in Edmunds county which had already seen minor water issues due to persistent early May rainfall. Some strong to high non-thunderstorm gusts also occurred with this system, including 66 mph in Agar and 57 mph in both the Polo and Summit areas.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A residence 2 miles northwest of Gary, SD had a barn roof damaged and a horse trailer rolled multiple times. A second residence, about 3 miles northwest of Gary, had a track of damage through their farm yard. An ash tree snapped at the trunk and fell on to the corner of the house. A single stall garage, which was bolted to the foundation, was lifted off the foundation and overturned. The vehicle in the garage was not damaged. A second garage to the north lost two thirds of its roof panels. Another machine shed|had a portion of the south wall pushed in. All debris from buildings was lofted to the northeast. A mostly empty grain bin was also ripped from the foundation and tossed across 485th Ave and into the neighboring field, coming to rest about 200 yards to|the northeast. Residents were without commercial power for two days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind was measured at the SDDOT RWIS station three miles south of Conde at 3am CDT. The roof was blown off a bar in Conde around this time as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A portion of western Dewey County was already in D2 at the start of August with a slow expansion and introduction of D3 conditions on August 16th. Precipitation varied across the county with 50 percent of normal for the highlighted drought area, though the southeast portion of the county received up to 3 times normal moisture for August.

Hot and humid air with highs in the 90s and dewpoints around 70 degrees set up across central and eastern South Dakota during the day Friday. This was just ahead of a front that was slowly migrating southeast, though the frontal passage brought little relief as temperatures on the north side of the front still topped out close to the century mark, with dewpoints remaining in the 60s. Morning lows both Friday and Saturday remained in the 70s for most of the area. A peak heat index value of 120 degrees was observed at weather stations in Mellette and Onaka, with over 70 weather stations in central, north central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index of 100 degrees or higher at some point during the day.

Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of an elongated area of low pressure during the evening of the 19th across central South Dakota, and then tracked north-northeast. Straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were produced, including at three miles south of Bunker (70 mph), and five miles west-southwest of Mound City (70 mph).

There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph was measured by South Dakota State University mesonet site FLNS2 3 miles north-northeast of Flandreau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system triggered several thunderstorms in a hot and favorable environment across north central South Dakota on the evening of the 23rd. The strongest of these storms produced large hail, ranging from nickel to two inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the hail, 50 to 60 mph wind gusts were estimated to have occurred.

Rainfall persisted across the area between April 28th and May 1st with three day totals over 3 inches between Edmunds, McPherson and Brown counties as well as upstream in North Dakota. This, combined with planned releases due to construction on the Elm Lake Spillway, resulted in minor flooding along the Elm River. Flooding was already occurring during the morning of May 1st and continued into May 2nd with a crest of 13.95 feet at the gauge near Frederick. The gauge at Westport went into flood stage on May 2nd, peaked in stage at 14.4 feet around mid day on May 4th, and then rapidly fell below flood stage on the morning of May 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elm River gauge near Westport entered flood stage on the afternoon of May 2nd,  peaked at 14.40 feet around mid day May 4nd and receded below flood stage in the early morning of May 5th. Agricultural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 47 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites SD605 near Parker, with a peak wind gust of 62 mph at 1251CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in the Missouri River Valley in the afternoon producing quarter to half dollar sized hail. These storms merged into a line which would eventually produce three weak tornadoes. One of these tornadoes destroyed a shed near Columbia. The system moved east of the James River Valley in the evening with limited severe weather thereafter. Additionally, heavy rain caused flooding in Edmunds county which had already seen minor water issues due to persistent early May rainfall. Some strong to high non-thunderstorm gusts also occurred with this system, including 66 mph in Agar and 57 mph in both the Polo and Summit areas.

A strong low pressure system tracked from western North Dakota into the southwestern Canadian Province of Manitoba during the morning hours of May 13th. Strong to high non-thunderstorm winds resulted during this time.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several eyewitnesses reported a small funnel/tornado descending, widening and developing very rapidly at the edge of Castlewood, SD. Damage was evident as soon as it reached the southern edge of town. A majority of the damage occurred between 3rd Avenue and 4th Avenue. On Market Street, a small funeral home visitation center was completely blown off its foundation. Debris from the funeral home was tossed to the north, including the air conditioning unit which was tossed into a yard one block to the north. A garage was destroyed and completely blown from its foundation on Allen Street. The next block to the north, a home lost a roof and sustained significant damage to external walls. Another garage on this street was completely blown off the foundation and destroyed. Similar damage was noted a block north on Main Street. Another home lost an entire roof and partial collapse of external walls. Yet another garage was destroyed as it slid off the foundation. The next block north, on Alice Street, significant roof damage was noted to several homes and another garage was completely shifted off of the foundation. One particular residence had a roof that appeared to be slightly rotated and was sagging. The Castlewood Elementary school, situated between Harry Street and Pear Street, sustained significant damage to the school and gymnasium roof. Damage was also sustained to sections of the brick external walls. The school bus shed was completely destroyed with loss of|the roof and external walls. North of the school, on Prairie Street, a residence sustained significant damage. At this property, a garage was completely destroyed, and a shed was tossed in the air landing upside down. The home itself lost the entire roof and the |south facing external walls collapsed. The tornado would continue to track north across an open field and across US Highway 22. Additional debris and tree damage was noted a mile north of US Highway 22 before the tornado lifted. Throughout the entire path of the tornado there were uprooted and snapped trees and significant siding and roof damage to homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from mPING stated that one inch tree limbs were broken and/or shingles were blown off.

Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones up to 2.0 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business in Arlington sustained significant roof damage. A nearby church also lost part of its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of an elongated area of low pressure during the evening of the 19th across central South Dakota, and then tracked north-northeast. Straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were produced, including at three miles south of Bunker (70 mph), and five miles west-southwest of Mound City (70 mph).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.

Hot and humid air with highs in the 90s and dewpoints around 70 degrees set up across central and eastern South Dakota during the day Friday. This was just ahead of a front that was slowly migrating southeast, though the frontal passage brought little relief as temperatures on the north side of the front still topped out close to the century mark, with dewpoints remaining in the 60s. Morning lows both Friday and Saturday remained in the 70s for most of the area. A peak heat index value of 120 degrees was observed at weather stations in Mellette and Onaka, with over 70 weather stations in central, north central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index of 100 degrees or higher at some point during the day.

Storms with supercell characteristics at times developed in the heat of the afternoon ahead of a cold front, and continued to expand in coverage through the evening and overnight. Up to tennis ball sized hail was observed near the Faulkton area, along with sporadic thunderstorm wind damage. Localized gusts up to 76 mph were observed at and near the Watertown airport as well. After thunderstorms had largely dissipated, environmental conditions became favorable for additional severe wind gusts of around 60 mph (gravity waves). Overall rainfall totals neared 4 inches in a few spots including 4 miles north of Vivian (3.82 inches) and Summit (3.98 inches). Minor street flooding was reported in Aberdeen.

A brief tornado also occurred near Wolsey, SD.||In the post warm frontal air mass from mid-evening into the early overnight of April 23, 2022, non-thunderstorm high winds produced multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph across southeast South Dakota.

Low pressure intensified while it moved from South Dakota into Minnesota by late on April 5, which slowed while moving into the western Great Lakes on April 7.  Strong cold advection and an intense pressure gradient produced periods of very strong synoptic winds across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD547 3 miles southeast of Montrose were from 40 to 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front draped from the upper James River Valley down into south central South Dakota triggered a severe thunderstorm during the early afternoon across north central South Dakota, and then a more significant and widespread bowing line of thunderstorms from central into northeastern South Dakota during the evening. The environment featured hot temperatures in the 80s and 90s and muggy conditions. The main thunderstorm complex produced straight-line winds of 60 to 85 mph, including a measured gust of 85 mph three miles south of Bunker, 82 mph ten miles southwest of the Chantler Creek Rec area, 80 mph eighteen miles south of Seneca, and 79 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport. There were several reports of property damage including numerous uprooted or damaged trees.

Evening storms developed across western and south central South Dakota before expanding north and east through the course of the night, either as individual cells or as small clusters. Hail and wind damage was isolated but spread across the area. The bowing segment of a thunderstorm produced an estimated 70 mph wind gust west of Trail City, which knocked down a Cottonwood tree. One of the stronger clusters also generated hail up to the size of golf balls north of Oacoma, while another cluster moved into far eastern South Dakota and produce hail to half dollar size in Lake Poinsett later that morning.

The James River continued to flood from May into and through June near Columbia, Stratford and Ashton, due to heavy rainfall. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded along with a few area roads, as river gauges reached moderate to major flood categories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Stratford was in moderate flood stage throughout June. It crested at 18.33 feet at 1am one June 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River was in minor flood stage near Castlewood from the end of May through June 4th, and in minor flood stage at Broadway Avenue in Watertown from the end of May through the morning of June 1st. The flooding was a result of prior heavy rainfall, and resulted in minor impacts to low-lying agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Castlewood was in minor flood stage from the end of May through the evening of June 4th. It crested at 10.68 feet at 5pm on June 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail measured two inches an hour after the storm had passed.

Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 48 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD527 near Forestburg, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 1240CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient resulted in isolated non-thunderstorm high winds across portions of southeast South Dakota during April 30, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Dakota Road Weather Information System site at the Platte-Winner Bridge (station SD554) briefly gusted to 63 mph around 1400 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the aftermath of a large, impactful storm system that moved across the region on April 22-23, 2022, a tight pressure gradient resulted in isolated non-thunderstorm high winds across portions of southeast South Dakota during April 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph most consistently during the morning hours, with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System at Fort Randall Dam (station SD588).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones up to 2.0 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed during the early morning hours of April 2nd across portions of southeast South Dakota. With temperatures at or slightly below freezing, slick spots developed on area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatality accident occurred approximately two miles west of De Smet, SD along U.S. Highway 14 near mile marker 375. The driver lost control due to icy conditions likely caused by dense fog and freezing temperatures in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553 4 miles west of Plankinton were from 40 to 47 mph, with a peak wind gust of 59 mph at 1613CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inch diameter tree branches were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly of nickel size, with a few stones as large as a quarter. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 90 mph. Nearby Lake Herman State Park was closed due to extensive tree damage, with an unoccupied camper crushed by a falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms redeveloped in the late morning, with supercell characteristics across eastern South Dakota producing up to one inch diameter hail and winds of 70 mph, in addition to 6 confirmed tornadoes, one of which was rated EF3. Heavy rain also occurred with this system, with the greatest amount of rainfall measured by a CoCoRaHS observer being 4.69 inches measured near Columbia, and the highest COOP observer report coming out of Milbank with a two day total of 2.86. This heavy rain resulted in flooding in several rural locations but also included parts of Watertown and Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in water flowing outside a shallow creek bed. This water approached homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid air with highs in the 90s and dewpoints around 70 degrees set up across central and eastern South Dakota during the day Friday. This was just ahead of a front that was slowly migrating southeast, though the frontal passage brought little relief as temperatures on the north side of the front still topped out close to the century mark, with dewpoints remaining in the 60s. Morning lows both Friday and Saturday remained in the 70s for most of the area. A peak heat index value of 120 degrees was observed at weather stations in Mellette and Onaka, with over 70 weather stations in central, north central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index of 100 degrees or higher at some point during the day.

An area of low pressure and associated frontal boundaries brought several severe thunderstorms to central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota from the evening of July 3rd to the early morning hours of July 4th. The storms produced high winds of 60 to 77 mph and torrential rainfall. The highest wind gusts include 77 mph one west-northwest of Mellette, 70 mph at Cravens Corner, and 14 miles north of Vivian. A member of the public and a trained spotter reported an estimated rainfall amount of 6 inches 5 miles north-northeast of Hosmer and 3 miles west of Hosmer. Some of the highest measured rainfall totals include 5.75 inches in Hosmer, 4.87 inches 3 miles northeast of Wetonka, 4.50 inches 5 west of Turton, 4.42 inches 6 miles northeast of Roscoe, 4.35 inches 6 southwest of Columbia, and 3.94 inches 8 miles north of Vivian.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Montana during the evening of July 4th then moved into northwestern South Dakota during the early morning hours of July 5th. The storms intensified around daybreak on July 5th, as they encountered a more favorable environment for severe storms. The storms started as supercells and then evolved into a derecho, with embedded supercells as they moved through Hand and Hyde counties. The significant severe storms tracked southeast across central South Dakota, into southeastern South Dakota, and eventually into northern Iowa while producing winds up to 91 mph and hail to the size of softballs in central South Dakota. The storms caused widespread damage to trees, grain bins, crops, and vehicles. Some of the highest wind gusts include 91 mph 4 miles north of Agar, 87 mph 1 mile north of Ree Heights, 84 mph 1 mile south-southwest of Timber Lake, and 77 mph 12 miles east-southeast of Sunshine Bible Academy. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage was done to numerous buildings in Dante. An 80 foot by 200 foot building and 42 foot by 60 foot building each had only one remaining wall standing. Numerous trees were uprooted, and several roofs were ripped off businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD597 1 mile west of Howard. A peak wind gust of 99 mph occurred at 1413CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple clusters of severe storms moved east and northeastward out of south central South Dakota, evolving into short bowing segments with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged a gas station canopy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused significant damage to the Armory roof in Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds within a wake low reached 40 to 50 mph, with a gust as high as 64 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD589 2 miles east of Dixon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding came to a quick end early in June 2022 as runoff from late May rainfall eased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of June 2022 on the Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2), with a crest at 0.31 feet above flood stage on June 1. The river fell below flood stage on June 4. A significant amount of rural land was flooded.

The storms produced high winds of 60 to 77 mph and torrential rainfall. The highest wind gusts include 77 mph one west-northwest of Mellette, 70 mph at Cravens Corner, and 14 miles north of Vivian. A member of the public and a trained spotter reported an estimated rainfall amount of 6 inches 5 miles north-northeast of Hosmer and 3 miles west of Hosmer. Some of the highest measured rainfall totals include 5.75 inches in Hosmer, 4.87 inches 3 miles northeast of Wetonka, 4.50 inches 5 west of Turton, 4.42 inches 6 miles northeast of Roscoe, 4.35 inches 6 southwest of Columbia, and 3.94 inches 8 miles north of Vivian.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 6 to 6.5 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter observed a tornado which developed west of 416th Avenue around 4 miles east-northeast of Yale, and then tracked to the east-northeast.  As the tornado crossed 416th Avenue, the tornado snapped four power poles.  After approximately another one-third mile across open farmland, the tornado lifted. The estimated maximum wind was 100 mph and the average path width was 40 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient behind a passing cold front produced strong northwest winds across much of western South Dakota. The strongest winds developed across northwestern South Dakota, where gusts around 65 mph were measured during the morning and early afternoon hours.
A thunderstorm became severe for a brief time across the central Black Hills, producing large hail in the Hill City area.
A thunderstorm became severe for a brief time across the central Black Hills, producing large hail in the Hill City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to ping pong ball size lasted around 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm over the northern Black Hills produced large hail in the Brownsville area.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Montana during the evening of July 4th then moved into northwestern South Dakota during the early morning hours of July 5th. The storms intensified around daybreak on July 5th, as they encountered a more favorable environment for severe storms. The storms started as supercells and then evolved into a derecho, with embedded supercells as they moved through Hand and Hyde counties. The significant severe storms tracked southeast across central South Dakota, into southeastern South Dakota, and eventually into northern Iowa while producing winds up to 91 mph and hail to the size of softballs in central South Dakota. The storms caused widespread damage to trees, grain bins, crops, and vehicles. Some of the highest wind gusts include 91 mph 4 miles north of Agar, 87 mph 1 mile north of Ree Heights, 84 mph 1 mile south-southwest of Timber Lake, and 77 mph 12 miles east-southeast of Sunshine Bible Academy. Hail, 4 inches in diameter, was reported in Promise and 2 miles north of Timber Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported an estimated wind gust of 60 mph.

Jones County was removed from the D2/severe drought at the start of July due to beneficial rainfall. However, southern Dewey and northern Stanley Counties still had D2/severe drought conditions. The drought remained unchanged until the last week in July, when northern Stanley County improved. A portion of Dewey County has been in D2/severe drought for 17 continuous months, the longest streak since records began in 2000. Pasture and range conditions continue to suffer in the pocket of remaining D2/severe drought.

A pair of thunderstorms brought severe weather over northeastern South Dakota during the evening hours of July 20th. The storms produced quarter-size hail and 60 to 66 mph winds. Several trees and power lines were downed in Sisseton. A tree branch, about an inch in diameter, went through a shed roof in Sisseton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a one-foot diameter tree limb, along with several other smaller limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD550 3 mile southeast of Jefferson. A peak wind gust of 65 mph occurred at 1532CST.

Due to above-normal rainfall, the James River continued to flood from June into and through July near Columbia, Stratford, and Ashton. Significant amounts of agricultural land was flooded along with a few area roads, as river gauges reached moderate to major flood categories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Columbia reached the July peak early in the month, with a slow decrease for the rest of the month. The river fell below the moderate stage on the 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Stratford reached a July peak of 17.84 feet on the 8th, with a slow decrease for the rest of the month. On Friday, July 22, an 11 year old girl was rescued from the James River after accidentally falling into the water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over three inches of rain fell in an hour over Ellsworth Air Force Base and parts of Box Elder. Runoff from the heavy rain flooded streets, homes, and businesses. Several streets were flooded on Ellsworth Air Force Base and throughout northern and eastern portions of Box Elder. At least 50 mobile homes were surrounded by water and ten to 15 homes were flooded with water up to six feet deep, mostly along the east side of North Ellsworth Road. Several businesses reported that their parking lots were flooded.

Estimated 3.50 inches of rain was reported at Bowdle, as well as 3 inches at Selby, Roscoe, and Miranda. Funnel clouds were reported near Highmore and Carpenter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public reported of a funnel cloud, 6 to 10 miles north and a little east of Highmore.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated 4.5 inches of rain, and it was still raining at the time of the report. As a result, there was street flooding in Roscoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD515 3 miles east of Cavour. A peak wind gust of 65 mph occurred at 1342CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the higher terrain of the High Plains and moved into central South Dakota during the evening of June 29th. Despite weak instability, deep inverted-V soundings resulted in DCAPE values of 1000-1500 J/kg, allowing for isolated damaging wind gusts with the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553 5 miles west of Plankinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding came to a quick end early in June 2022 as runoff from late May rainfall eased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of June 2022 on the Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2), but quickly fell below flood stage on June 1. A minor amount of rural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm became anchored over the Box Elder area for close to an hour. The storm was severe at times, producing hail and wind gusts to 70 mph. Heavy rainfall of two to over three inches in Box Elder produced flooding and areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over three inches of rain fell in an hour over Ellsworth Air Force Base and parts of Box Elder.  Runoff from heavy rain flooded streets, homes, and businesses. Several streets were flooded on Ellsworth Air Force Base, Antelope Ridge, and throughout northern and eastern portions of Box Elder.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Montana during the evening of July 4th then moved into northwestern South Dakota during the early morning hours of July 5th. The storms intensified around daybreak on July 5th, as they encountered a more favorable environment for severe storms. The storms started as supercells and then evolved into a derecho, with embedded supercells as they moved through Hand and Hyde counties. The significant severe storms tracked southeast across central South Dakota, into southeastern South Dakota, and eventually into northern Iowa while producing winds up to 91 mph and hail to the size of softballs in central South Dakota. The storms caused widespread damage to trees, grain bins, crops, and vehicles. Some of the highest wind gusts include 91 mph 4 miles north of Agar, 87 mph 1 mile north of Ree Heights, 84 mph 1 mile south-southwest of Timber Lake, and 77 mph 12 miles east-southeast of Sunshine Bible Academy. Hail, 4 inches in diameter, was reported in Promise and 2 miles north of Timber Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts. Some enhanced non-thunderstorm wind gusts occurred with initial showers in the lower Missouri River Valley in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up in an open field 4 miles south-southwest of Volga, west of 463rd Avenue and south of 214th St.  The tornado moved generally eastward for around one-half mile, before it impacted a farmstead and caused considerable damage to the barn. The tornado then moved across 463rd Avenue into an open field and dissipated after another one-third mile. The estimated maximum wind speed was 95 mph and the average path width was 40 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dime to quarter size hail occurred just north of Brookings.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous 3 inch tree limbs were broken off by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central South Dakota along a warm front. An isolated severe storm produced wind damage to a mature tree one-mile south-southwest of Ree Heights.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported damage to a large portion of a mature tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts at near 70 mph.

Monsoonal moisture streaming into the area, combined with slow moving thunderstorms, produced a narrow area of heavy rain from Black Hawk to Rapid Valley. Runoff from this rainfall caused minor street flooding on a few city streets in Rapid City, flooded open agricultural fields in Rapid Valley, and caused high flows and minor flooding along Rapid Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell across a narrow area from Black Hawk to Rapid Valley in about two hours. Runoff from this rain caused minor street flooding on the east side of Rapid City as the drainage system in town were overwhelmed. Open agricultural fields in Rapid Valley were flooded with a few inches of water as well. The abundant runoff also caused high flows and minor flooding along Rapid Creek from the Central States Fairgrounds to Bradsky Road.

Hot temperatures in the 90s and lower 100s, along with dew point temperatures in the 60s and lower 70s, produced heat indices of 100 to near 110 degrees during the afternoon of July 18th. Some activities were postponed or canceled due to the heat. Some of the highest heat indices include 109 degrees in Ridgeview, 108 degrees in Hayes and McLaughlin, and 107 degrees in Pierre.

An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 72 mph was measured by South Dakota State University mesonet site SFWS2 3 miles southeast of Wall Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD532 2 miles west-southwest of Gayville. A peak wind gust of 65 mph occurred at 1428CST.
