EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

An isolated thunderstorm developed across northeast Nebraska and wandered into southeast South Dakota. With 500-1000 J/kg of elevated MUCAPE, there was a brief window for spotty large hail.
High flow continued as a result of heavier May rainfall in upper portions of the basin, with a mid-month crest in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of June 2022 on the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2), with a crest at 2.15 feet above flood stage on June 13. A significant amount of rural land was flooded. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and associated frontal boundaries brought several severe thunderstorms to central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota from the evening of July 3rd to the early morning hours of July 4th. The storms produced high winds of 60 to 77 mph and torrential rainfall. The highest wind gusts include 77 mph one west-northwest of Mellette, 70 mph at Cravens Corner, and 14 miles north of Vivian. A member of the public and a trained spotter reported an estimated rainfall amount of 6 inches 5 miles north-northeast of Hosmer and 3 miles west of Hosmer. Some of the highest measured rainfall totals include 5.75 inches in Hosmer, 4.87 inches 3 miles northeast of Wetonka, 4.50 inches 5 west of Turton, 4.42 inches 6 miles northeast of Roscoe, 4.35 inches 6 southwest of Columbia, and 3.94 inches 8 miles north of Vivian.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe over northwestern Jackson County, producing wind gusts around 60 mph in the Cottonwood area.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the eastern slopes of the central Black Hills, producing large hail near Hayward.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe over the northeastern slopes of the Black Hills, producing large hail in the Sturgis area.
A severe thunderstorm over northeastern Wyoming weakened as it moved into the northern Black Hills and produced nickel sized hail west of Spearfish.

June began with D2/severe drought conditions across portions of Dewey, Stanley and Jones Counties, and finished with a modest improvement as Jones County transitioned into D1/moderate drought mid-month. This was due to several rounds of thunderstorms, which totaled to as much as two inches above normal for the month across portions of central South Dakota, though rainfall was sometimes spotty. Near to only slightly above normal temperatures were recorded as well. Portions of Dewey County have now been in D2/severe drought for 16 continuous months, which is the longest streak since records began in 2000. Pasture and range conditions continue to suffer in the pocket of  remaining D2/severe drought.

Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by law enforcement and trained spotters, estimated 80 mph wind gusts damaged several buildings and trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.

Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.

The storms primarily produced large hail initially, then strong wind gusts and hail accompanied the storms as they moved toward central South Dakota.

Severe thunderstorms developed in Montana during the evening of July 4th then moved into northwestern South Dakota during the early morning hours of July 5th. The storms intensified around daybreak on July 5th, as they encountered a more favorable environment for severe storms. The storms started as supercells and then evolved into a derecho, with embedded supercells as they moved through Hand and Hyde counties. The significant severe storms tracked southeast across central South Dakota, into southeastern South Dakota, and eventually into northern Iowa while producing winds up to 91 mph and hail to the size of softballs in central South Dakota. The storms caused widespread damage to trees, grain bins, crops, and vehicles. Some of the highest wind gusts include 91 mph 4 miles north of Agar, 87 mph 1 mile north of Ree Heights, 84 mph 1 mile south-southwest of Timber Lake, and 77 mph 12 miles east-southeast of Sunshine Bible Academy. Hail, 4 inches in diameter, was reported in Promise and 2 miles north of Timber Lake.

An area of low pressure and associated cold front initiated a vigorous complex of severe thunderstorms across western South Dakota during the evening of June 12th, and these storms tracked east-southeast to affect south central SD after sunset and into the early morning of the 13th. These storms produced destructive straight-line winds of, based on damage assessments, up to 115-130 mph in Jones County, as well as three tornadoes, two of which were rated EF-2 in Jones County following the destruction of property on multiple farmsteads. Additional thunderstorms then redeveloped and reached or nearly reached severe limits across portions of central South Dakota, and training storms brought heavy rainfall of locally 3 inches or more in and around the Pierre and Mobridge areas. Finally, intense non-thunderstorm winds developed on the backside of this system during the early morning hours of the 13th, and impacted much of central and northeastern South Dakota. Some of the peak measures wind gusts include 78 mph five miles SSW of Miranda, 75 mph three miles south of Conde (the roof of a bar was blown off in Conde), and 73 mph six miles SSW of Peever. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touchdown ripped five large grain bins from their base. The bins were well anchored, in fact, large pieces of cement remained attached to the pins when they were pulled out. The bins were projectiled into two machine sheds and eventually carried about a half mile to the east southeast. Other debris, including a water tank and a dumpster were also tossed east. Other farm equipment was also damaged, including a tipped trailer and displaced grain auger. Pressure change inside the machine sheds also resulted in several combine windshields to be popped out. The house and machine sheds on the property sustained very little wind damage but were built well above normal standards, rated to withstand wind speeds at thresholds of 135 mph and 120 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD547 3 miles southeast of Montrose. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up just north of the intersection of 41st Street and Western Avenue, where it uprooted a large tree. The tornado travelled generally eastward for around one-half mile before it dissipated one block south of the Augustana University athletic fields. Two garage structures collapsed along the track, with some roof damage to adjacent homes. Numerous trees were topped or uprooted, with one tree falling on an unoccupied car. The estimated maximum wind speed was 95 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

An area of low pressure and associated frontal boundaries brought several severe thunderstorms to central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota from the evening of July 3rd to the early morning hours of July 4th. The storms produced high winds of 60 to 77 mph and torrential rainfall. The highest wind gusts include 77 mph one west-northwest of Mellette, 70 mph at Cravens Corner, and 14 miles north of Vivian. A member of the public and a trained spotter reported an estimated rainfall amount of 6 inches 5 miles north-northeast of Hosmer and 3 miles west of Hosmer. Some of the highest measured rainfall totals include 5.75 inches in Hosmer, 4.87 inches 3 miles northeast of Wetonka, 4.50 inches 5 west of Turton, 4.42 inches 6 miles northeast of Roscoe, 4.35 inches 6 southwest of Columbia, and 3.94 inches 8 miles north of Vivian.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported via social media flash flooding due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed with a strengthening low-level jet during the overnight hours.  With MUCAPE of around 1000 J/kg and 30 knots of effective shear, isolated storms produced up to quarter size hail.

A weak synoptic-scale wave progressed through the area atop a flat upper-level ridge, initiating several clusters of thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.  Storms developed near a weak convergence zone, in an environment of 1000-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and marginally strong deep-layer shear of 30 knots.  Mainly multi-cell clusters resulted in marginally severe damaging wind gusts due to sub-cloud dryness and marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven power poles along South Dakota Highway 34 were damaged by thunderstorm winds, six of those snapped.

With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was torn off of a building by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were snapped by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed approximately two miles east of Brandon, just south of State Highway 138 west of 484th Avenue.  The tornado moved eastward and toppled several large trees shortly after touchdown, then turned to more of a east-southeast direction. After tracking about one-quarter mile, the tornado did the greatest damage along its track at the Beaver Valley Lutheran Church, where a large portion of the roof was removed.  The tornado continued another couple tenths of a mile across open fields before it dissipated. The estimated maximum wind speed was 105 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of eastward-moving strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 29th. The first was associated with weak low pressure across south central South Dakota, and the second was triggered by a cold front across north central South Dakota, both in hot environments with daytime highs in the 90s. These storms produced 60 to 70 mph or greater wind gusts, including 68 mph six miles southwest of the Joe Creek Rec Area, and damaged trees and other property across the area. High non-thunderstorm winds developed across east central South Dakota during the evening as well, the highest measured being 63 mph four miles west of Bryant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in Montana during the evening of July 4th then moved into northwestern South Dakota during the early morning hours of July 5th. The storms intensified around daybreak on July 5th, as they encountered a more favorable environment for severe storms. The storms started as supercells and then evolved into a derecho, with embedded supercells as they moved through Hand and Hyde counties. The significant severe storms tracked southeast across central South Dakota, into southeastern South Dakota, and eventually into northern Iowa while producing winds up to 91 mph and hail to the size of softballs in central South Dakota. The storms caused widespread damage to trees, grain bins, crops, and vehicles. Some of the highest wind gusts include 91 mph 4 miles north of Agar, 87 mph 1 mile north of Ree Heights, 84 mph 1 mile south-southwest of Timber Lake, and 77 mph 12 miles east-southeast of Sunshine Bible Academy. Hail, 4 inches in diameter, was reported in Promise and 2 miles north of Timber Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported extensive damage to a large outdoor storage building due to high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed with a strengthening low-level jet during the overnight hours.  With MUCAPE of around 1000 J/kg and 30 knots of effective shear, isolated storms produced up to quarter size hail.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 97 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KMDS 1 mile east-northeast of Madison.  Extensive tree and building damage occurred throughout the city.  Five families were displaced as damage to their houses from wind and falling trees rendered residences uninhabitable.  Several 500 lb. tanks from an agricultural supply story were blown across a four lane highway into the water treatment plant. Three of the tanks ended up in a creek by the water treatment plant, with one severely damaging the cooperative observation equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022, with a slow fall in river levels. However, heavy rainfall which totaled 5 to 7 inches fell in a very small portion of the mid to upper-James River Basin during the first week of July and resulted in a mid- to late-month crest before another slow fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of July 2022 on the James River near Mitchell (gage site JRMS2). Five to seven inches of rainfall the first seven days of July in a small area at and north of Huron resulted in a crest at 1.20 feet above flood stage on July 24. A significant amount of rural land was flooded. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and large branches downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures in the 90s and lower 100s, along with dew point temperatures in the 60s and lower 70s, produced heat indices of 100 to near 110 degrees during the afternoon of July 18th. Some activities were postponed or canceled due to the heat. Some of the highest heat indices include 109 degrees in Ridgeview, 108 degrees in Hayes and McLaughlin, and 107 degrees in Pierre.
A very small band of heavy rainfall from 3 to 5 inches fell on July 5 in lower portions of the Split Rock Creek Basin north of Brandon and Corson, resulting in a brief episode of minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 3 to 5 inches fell between Garretson and Brandon in the Split Rock Creek Basin. Rapid runoff resulted in brief minor flooding from late on July 5 through early July 6, as Split Rock Creek near Corson (gage site COSS2) crested 1.49 feet above flood stage at 2300CST July 5. A significant amount of low-lying rural land was flooded.

June began with D2/severe drought conditions across portions of Dewey, Stanley and Jones Counties, and finished with a modest improvement as Jones County transitioned into D1/moderate drought mid-month. This was due to several rounds of thunderstorms, which totaled to as much as two inches above normal for the month across portions of central South Dakota, though rainfall was sometimes spotty. Near to only slightly above normal temperatures were recorded as well. Portions of Dewey County have now been in D2/severe drought for 16 continuous months, which is the longest streak since records began in 2000. Pasture and range conditions continue to suffer in the pocket of  remaining D2/severe drought.

June began with D2/severe drought conditions across portions of Dewey, Stanley and Jones Counties, and finished with a modest improvement as Jones County transitioned into D1/moderate drought mid-month. This was due to several rounds of thunderstorms, which totaled to as much as two inches above normal for the month across portions of central South Dakota, though rainfall was sometimes spotty. Near to only slightly above normal temperatures were recorded as well. Portions of Dewey County have now been in D2/severe drought for 16 continuous months, which is the longest streak since records began in 2000. Pasture and range conditions continue to suffer in the pocket of  remaining D2/severe drought.
Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with a continuation of well-below normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Canton was around 50 percent of normal during November. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds in Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In response to a strong north-northeastward moving low pressure system, thunderstorm developed in a favorable environment over south central South Dakota just after midnight on the 14th, and quickly became severe with large hail and straight-line wind. Winds became increasingly extreme as they tracked northeast through the early morning, peaking between 4 and 5 AM across portions of Edmunds, McPherson and Brown Counties. Two separate personal weather stations recorded 120 mph wind gusts, one to the northwest of Roscoe and the other west of Frederick. Damage was widespread, including numerous downed trees and power lines, widespread roof, outbuilding and grain bin damage, and 16 steel towers were downed about 7 miles west-southwest of Frederick, which is indicative of 130 mph wind speeds. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

Surface-based thunderstorms developed across north-central Nebraska, and moved east-northeast along the Missouri River.  With up to 3000 J/kg of CAPE and deep-layer shear of 30 to 35 knots, spotty marginally large hail fell with stronger updrafts.
Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with a continuation of well-below normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highly variable precipitation occurred during November, which ranged from less than 25 percent of normal near Iroquois, to around normal near Wessington.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into eastern and northeastern South Dakota. This thunderstorm complex generated 14 total tornadoes across northeastern South Dakota in addition to a broad area of straight-line wind damage with measured speeds up to 102 mph in Gary, SD. The damage swath was so large from this system that it encompassed most of northeastern South Dakota and western Minnesota, with damage to a countless number of homes and trees. The most impactful tornado was an EF-2 which damaged numerous homes in the town of Castlewood and drew national media attention. Governor Kristi Noem requested a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which was later granted, and signed Executive Order 2022-06 to help residents recover from related storm damage. Estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. South Dakota National Guard activated personnel to help with clear debris and provide security for the town of Castlewood during cleanup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 70 mph winds damaged trees.

A 24-hour rainfall record of 5.67 inches was established at the Sioux Falls Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was ongoing at 6th and Cliff Ave.

These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts, estimated at a peak from 80 to 90 mph, caused significant tree damage. A dock in Wall Lake was also flipped on its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted with several other snapped by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In response to a strong north-northeastward moving low pressure system, thunderstorm developed in a favorable environment over south central South Dakota just after midnight on the 14th, and quickly became severe with large hail and straight-line wind. Winds became increasingly extreme as they tracked northeast through the early morning, peaking between 4 and 5 AM across portions of Edmunds, McPherson and Brown Counties. Two separate personal weather stations recorded 120 mph wind gusts, one to the northwest of Roscoe and the other west of Frederick. Damage was widespread, including numerous downed trees and power lines, widespread roof, outbuilding and grain bin damage, and 16 steel towers were downed about 7 miles west-southwest of Frederick, which is indicative of 130 mph wind speeds. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening across central South Dakota, in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment. Large hail was the main hazard, with stones ranging from quarter to tennis ball size. A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms then developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening, and these produced straight-line wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph.

Gusty south to southeast winds developed across much of western South Dakota during the daytime hours. The strongest winds were noted across portions of the far western South Dakota plains, especially far southwestern South Dakota, where some gusts around 65 mph were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked from the northern Rockies through the northern Plains, producing a wintry mix of precipitation across the region; including freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet, and snow. The storm brought the heaviest snow across northwestern South Dakota, where two to seven inches fell in many areas. Local amounts near one foot were reported near and along the North Dakota border, especially in the Lemmon area. Gusty winds developed on the back side of the system, producing considerable blowing and drifting snow, with greatly reduced visibilities at times. Snow amounts further south across western South Dakota were mostly two inches or less; with some freezing rain, sleet, and snow across south central South Dakota.

An active pattern brought a series of disturbances across the area, producing periods of snow across the northern into central Black Hills area. The strongest disturbance passed through the area during the overnight and daytime hours of the 15th, producing areas of moderate to heavy snow. The highest snowfall totals overall on the 14th and 15th were across the northern Black Hills, where over a foot was recorded in favored higher elevations from Lead to Cheyenne Crossing and O'Neill Pass. Narrow bands of heavy snow and high snow ratios produced some localized amounts of six inches or more, especially across the adjacent foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern brought a series of disturbances across the area, producing periods of snow across the northern into central Black Hills area. The strongest disturbance passed through the area during the overnight and daytime hours of the 15th, producing areas of moderate to heavy snow. The highest snowfall totals overall on the 14th and 15th were across the northern Black Hills, where over a foot was recorded in favored higher elevations from Lead to Cheyenne Crossing and O'Neill Pass. Narrow bands of heavy snow and high snow ratios produced some localized amounts of six inches or more, especially across the adjacent foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In response to a strong north-northeastward moving low pressure system, thunderstorm developed in a favorable environment over south central South Dakota just after midnight on the 14th, and quickly became severe with large hail and straight-line wind. Winds became increasingly extreme as they tracked northeast through the early morning, peaking between 4 and 5 AM across portions of Edmunds, McPherson and Brown Counties. Two separate personal weather stations recorded 120 mph wind gusts, one to the northwest of Roscoe and the other west of Frederick. Damage was widespread, including numerous downed trees and power lines, widespread roof, outbuilding and grain bin damage, and 16 steel towers were downed about 7 miles west-southwest of Frederick, which is indicative of 130 mph wind speeds. A Presidential Disaster Declaration was requested and then granted for six counties across South Dakota, Jones and Spink among them, to help residents recover from storm damage that took place from June 11-14th. Initial estimated statewide damage to public infrastructure is assessed at 1.6 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteen steel towers were downed by significant straight-line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front.  The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and a transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temporary roof was blown off a school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved through the region; bringing colder temperatures, gusty winds, and areas of snow. The heaviest snow was associated with upslope enhancement over the northern Black Hills. Snowfall of four to eight inches were noted during the overnight and morning hours over the favored areas from Lead to Terry Peak and the Cheyenne Crossing area. Gusty winds produced some blowing and drifting snow, with reduced visibilities at times.
Strong southwest downslope winds developed during the late evening in the Spearfish area, producing a few wind gusts around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th.  With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward.  Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several 3 to 6 inch diameter limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to above-normal rainfall, the James River continued to flood from June into and through July near Columbia, Stratford, and Ashton. Significant amounts of agricultural land was flooded along with a few area roads, as river gauges reached moderate to major flood categories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Friday, July 22nd, a 13-year-old girl was swept downstream while swimming in the James River near Frankfort. The search was postponed around 11:30 p.m. Friday due to the dangers of the current and the inability to see in the dark. The girl's body was found around 9:25 a.m. on Saturday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with a continuation of well-below normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at White Lake was 25 to 50 percent of normal during November. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and brief light freezing rain developed on the cold side of a nearly stationary frontal boundary as surface low pressure moved from Colorado to Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light accumulation of freezing rain and freezing drizzle to around one-tenth of an inch occurred at ASOS site KHON in Huron.  Area roads became slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continued in western Dewey County and expanded back into north central and northeast South Dakota for the start of the month in response to an extremely dry September and October that featured less than 25 percent normal precipitation and above normal temperatures for both months. Some relief did occur for eastern South Dakota around mid-November, with overall a slightly above-normal precipitation total for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive ridge of high pressure aloft forced a strong warm plume to the northeast, ahead of a frontal boundary on August 5.  The combination of temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and dew point readings in the mid 60s to around 70 led to heat index values from 105-110 for several hours across south central and southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 90s and dew point readings in the upper 60s resulted in heat index values as high as 105 degrees 10 miles west of Geddes, 109 degrees at Wagner, and 111 degrees at Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface-based thunderstorms developed across north-central Nebraska, and moved east-northeast along the Missouri River.  With up to 3000 J/kg of CAPE and deep-layer shear of 30 to 35 knots, spotty marginally large hail fell with stronger updrafts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several limbs up to 4 inches in diameter, as well as a 12-inch diameter tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive ridge of high pressure aloft forced a strong warm plume to the northeast, ahead of a frontal boundary on August 5.  The combination of temperatures in the mid to upper 90s and dew point readings in the mid 60s to around 70 led to heat index values from 105-110 for several hours across south central and southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 90s and dew point readings in the mid to upper 60s resulted in heat index values as high as 108 degrees 6 miles south-southwest of Utica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northeast to southwest-oriented cold front triggered several thunderstorms across east central South Dakota during the evening hours, as it tracked east into warm, humid air. These thunderstorms quickly became severe by producing quarter to half dollar sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time of the report was radar-estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass settled over the region, bringing bitter cold temperatures and gusty winds that produced wind chills of 30 below to 55 below zero at times from late day on the 20th through the 23rd. A period of light snow developed across much of the area on the 21st, bringing light accumulations and blowing snow, greatly reducing visibility at times through the 22nd.

Pockets of showers and thunderstorms developed across northeastern South Dakota as an area of low pressure tracked northeast during the overnight of the 14th into the 15th. These thunderstorms produced isolated severe thunderstorm wind gusts around 60 mph at the Summit and Peever SDDOT stations, and also damaged trees on the northwest side of Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was recorded at mesonet station 143 six miles south-southwest of Peever.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding from snowmelt and rainfall earlier this spring continued into and then ended in September due to dry conditions. The James River at the Columbia river gauge, CMBS2, was in minor flood stage (between 13 and 16 feet) for about 2 hours on the early morning of September 1st prior to falling below flood stage thereafter. It had been in flood stage since April 28th. Additionally, minor flooding (between 14 and 17 feet) persisted from September 1st through the early morning of September 23rd at the Stratford river gauge, SFDS2. It had been in flood stage since May 7th. Impacts were predominantly to agricultural lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the James River was recorded by the river gauge at Stratford, SFDS2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level frontogenetic zone drifted eastward with an upper-level trough and  produced a band of snowfall from 2 to 5 inches across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Dell Rapids, and between 3.0 and 3.5 inches at Hartford, Garretson, and around the Sioux Falls metro area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.9 inches at Tea, 3.8 inches in southern Sioux Falls, and 3.2 inches in Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across southern Canada generated strong winds that topped out around 50 to 60+ mph. A peak wind of 66 mph was reported in Frederick, and a 63 mph gust was observed at Peever. These strong winds combined with dry conditions to help generate a large wildfire in Edmunds County, as well as several other smaller fires scattered across northeast South Dakota that were quickly suppressed. Blowing dust was also noted on satellite, and the combination of smoke and dust coloring the sky gathered attention on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated thunderstorm development on an east-west boundary across the Sioux Falls metro area produced a localized 3 to 6 inches of rainfall in a few hours.  Flooding impacts were mainly felt in areas of rapid urban runoff, with other surrounding less-urbanized areas in severe to extreme drought able to avoid any flooding. A 24-hour rainfall record of 5.67 inches was established at the Sioux Falls Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple vehicles were stranded in high water in the streets of northern and western Sioux Falls.

A low pressure system tracked from Colorado through southeast South Dakota. Initially, a period of freezing drizzle resulted in hazardous travel conditions and at least one request for No Travel Advised early on the morning of the 9th between the Missouri and James River Valleys. Temperatures briefly moderated during the day for a few locations, but then fell back to near or below freezing during the course of the afternoon and evening on the 9th. The main push of moisture, which involved a mix of steady freezing rain and occasional sleet, developed during the evening of the 9th and moved north through the overnight hours. Thunder was noted in several areas, including the cities of Pierre, Aberdeen, and Miller. Widespread accumulations of freezing drizzle and freezing rain totaled at between a quarter inch and one half inch of ice, with an estimated one inch of total ice accumulation in Gettysburg. Winds were also gusty behind the system, with peak winds of 56 mph in Altamont, 50 mph at Brandt, and 49 mph at Summit.|The combination of ice and strong winds that followed resulted in widespread tree damage and power interruptions between the Missouri and James River Valleys. According to the Brown County Emergency Manager, approximately 1,000 customers were without power in just Brown County at one time, and around 80 remained without power on the morning of the 12th. Well below average temperatures moved in behind this system, which only prolonged impacts as temperatures remained below 32�� F for many days, preventing a natural thaw of the ice and snow. |The surface observing system at the Aberdeen regional airport lost power on the 10th at 14:00 CST and remained inoperable through the afternoon of the 11th of November. The city forester in Aberdeen estimates 1000 manhours devoted to storm damage cleanup, with about 35 percent of trees damaged from the storm. |Additionally, freezing drizzle changed over to heavy snow in far north central South Dakota, with 13 inches of total snow reported 6 miles southeast of McIntosh, and 6 inches in Pollock.

The main push of moisture, which involved a mix of steady freezing rain and occasional sleet, developed during the evening of the 9th and moved north through the overnight hours. Thunder was noted in several areas, including the cities of Pierre, Aberdeen, and Miller. Widespread accumulations of freezing drizzle and freezing rain totaled at between a quarter inch and one half inch of ice, with an estimated one inch of total ice accumulation in Gettysburg. Winds were also gusty behind the system, with peak winds of 56 mph in Altamont, 50 mph at Brandt, and 49 mph at Summit.|The combination of ice and strong winds that followed resulted in widespread tree damage and power interruptions between the Missouri and James River Valleys. According to the Brown County Emergency Manager, approximately 1,000 customers were without power in just Brown County at one time, and around 80 remained without power on the morning of the 12th. Well below average temperatures moved in behind this system, which only prolonged impacts as temperatures remained below 32�� F for many days, preventing a natural thaw of the ice and snow. |The surface observing system at the Aberdeen regional airport lost power on the 10th at 14:00 CST and remained inoperable through the afternoon of the 11th of November. The city forester in Aberdeen estimates 1000 manhours devoted to storm damage cleanup, with about 35 percent of trees damaged from the storm. |Additionally, freezing drizzle changed over to heavy snow in far north central South Dakota, with 13 inches of total snow reported 6 miles southeast of McIntosh, and 6 inches in Pollock.

Repeated thunderstorm development on an east-west boundary across the Sioux Falls metro area produced a localized 3 to 6 inches of rainfall in a few hours.  Flooding impacts were mainly felt in areas of rapid urban runoff, with other surrounding less-urbanized areas in severe to extreme drought able to avoid any flooding. A 24-hour rainfall record of 5.67 inches was established at the Sioux Falls Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding was present at Interstate 229 and 10th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from Colorado through southeast South Dakota, bringing a wintry mix initially to north central South Dakota late Monday before changing to heavy snow early Tuesday morning. Snow accumulated 13 inches in total 6 miles southeast of McIntosh and 6 inches in Pollock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm outbuilding wall was blown out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds developed across portions of the Black Hills. The strongest winds developed during the early evening, especially in the Lead area, where some gusts of 60 to 75 mph were recorded.

A quick moving disturbance passed across the central Plains. The system clipped far southwestern and south central South Dakota, bringing accumulating snow. Amounts of two to five inches were common across these areas, with some higher amounts across southern Todd County.

A clipper system brought gusty northwest winds to much of the area. The strongest winds developed across portions of the northwestern to west central South Dakota plains, where some gusts of 60 to 70 mph developed from the midday into the evening hours.

While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite just slightly above normal temperatures, precipitation at White Lake was less than 25 percent of normal during October. Severe (D2) drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) conditions early in the month.

At the beginning of October, only 3.26 percent of Aberdeen���s forecast area experienced D2 Severe Drought. The area included southwest Dewey and far northwest Stanley County, the bootheel of Lyman County, far eastern Hand, far southern Spink, and the extreme southwest corner of Clark County. The month featured one to three degrees above average temperatures and one to two inches below normal precipitation. These conditions and periods of high winds caused D2 Severe Drought to expand into northeastern South Dakota, with 12.39 percent of Aberdeen���s forecast area experiencing D2 Severe Drought by October 25th. The National Agricultural Statistics Service showed topsoil moisture rated at 80 percent short or very short. The limited soil moisture, high winds, and harvest season created poor air quality at times.

A tight surface pressure gradient over the region produced high winds across central and north-central South Dakota during the afternoon and evening hours of October 13th. The high winds and low humidity combined to produce high grassland fire danger in central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota. In addition, the winds and harvest season caused poor air quality. Some of the highest wind gusts include 72 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 65 mph 4 miles north of Agar, and 64 mph near Stephan.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous 3 to 4 inch diameter tree limbs.

Pockets of D2/Severe Drought persisted from November into the first half of December, due to below normal amounts of precipitation. However, a large winter storm system from December 12-16th produced widespread heavy snow with between 0.5 and 2.5 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation. With the exception of portions of north central South Dakota, this resulted in an improvement in drought conditions to D1/Moderate Drought or D0/Abnormally Dry for all areas, including across far southwestern Dewey County which had been in at least D2/Severe Drought since February 16th, 2021.

A powerful winter storm moved slowly across the central and northern Plains to the upper Midwest; bringing heavy snow, some mixed precipitation, strong winds, and periods of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across the plains for a multi-day period. Snowfall was heaviest from northwestern to south central South Dakota and across the northern Black Hills. Precipitation started as a mix in the form of rain, freezing rain, and freezing drizzle across much of the plains, then changed to snow in most areas by the daytime hours on the 13th. Bands of snow were moderate to heavy at times in many areas, with a very prolonged period of upslope enhanced snowfall across the northern Black Hills. Amounts across the area ranged from one foot to over two feet across much of the plains, with the highest amounts generally from Oglala Lakota County into parts of south central South Dakota. Lesser amounts were noted over portions of southwestern South Dakota, from Rapid City and the central Black Hills to Fall River County, due to predominant downslope winds. The highest amounts were noted across the northern Black Hills area, where 18 to 36 inches were recorded in many areas, with favored locations across the higher elevations received three to four feet or more over several days. Wind gusts of 40 to over 60 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting snow, with drifts to ten feet noted on the plains. Interstate 90 was closed from the Wyoming/South Dakota state line to the Missouri River for more than three days, with travel on many secondary roads becoming very difficult to impossible on the plains and the northern Black Hills area. Six deaths reported in Todd and Mellette counties, mostly due to medical emergencies in which emergency services were unable to reach these people in time. However, two people died due to hypothermia from exposure to the cold.

Interstate 90 was closed from the Wyoming/South Dakota state line to the Missouri River for more than three days, with travel on many secondary roads becoming very difficult to impossible on the plains and the northern Black Hills area. Six deaths reported in Todd and Mellette counties, mostly due to medical emergencies in which emergency services were unable to reach these people in time. However, two people died due to hypothermia from exposure to the cold.

A powerful winter storm moved slowly across the central and northern Plains to the upper Midwest; bringing heavy snow, some mixed precipitation, strong winds, and periods of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across the plains for a multi-day period. Snowfall was heaviest from northwestern to south central South Dakota and across the northern Black Hills. A strong cold front brought high winds to central and north-central South Dakota during the afternoon and evening hours of October 12th. The high winds and low humidity combined to produce very high grassland fire danger in central, north-central, and northeastern South Dakota. In addition, the winds and harvest season caused poor air quality. Some of the highest wind gusts include 72 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 69 mph at Lees Corner and the Pierre Municipal Airport, and 66 mph at Trail City and Timber Lake.

The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to one-half inch on elevated and road surfaces at Hetland.

A fire ignited about 6 miles east of Fort Thompson during the afternoon on October 10th. The fire spread northward due to strong southerly winds and low relative humidity. No structures were reportedly lost. The fire burned an estimated 3,000 acres.

Surface low pressure across Montana and high pressure across southern Illinois resulted in a tight pressure gradient and thus strong southerly wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph across portions of central and northeastern South Dakota during the morning hours of Thursday, December 1st. The strongest gust of 59 mph was recorded from an SDDOT weather station across the relatively higher terrain near Ree Heights.

Pockets of D2/Severe Drought persisted from November into the first half of December, due to below normal amounts of precipitation. However, a large winter storm system from December 12-16th produced widespread heavy snow with between 0.5 and 2.5 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation. With the exception of portions of north central South Dakota, this resulted in an improvement in drought conditions to D1/Moderate Drought or D0/Abnormally Dry for all areas, including across far southwestern Dewey County which had been in at least D2/Severe Drought since February 16th, 2021.

With only between a half an inch to an inch of total precipitation throughout the month (about half an inch to an inch below normal) and ending 4 to 5 degrees above normal on the whole, far western Dewey County remained in D2 drought throughout September. Impacts continue to include some stock ponds and dugouts that remain dry or contain water of poor quality, dry topsoil and subsoils, and very poor pasture and range conditions. Grassland fire danger has been heightened as well.

Medical professionals and police determined that a toddler had died due to heat after being left in a vehicle in the Clark Elementary School parking lot. The child was discovered around 5pm. The high temperature in Clark was 89 degrees, as recorded by the Clark COOP Observer, which is about 15 degrees above normal for September 6th.

The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to around one-quarter inch on elevated and road surfaces at Flandreau.  Slick conditions resulted in several accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm outbuilding door and wall were blown out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry weather resulted in a gradual drop in water levels along the James River through the month, and flooding ended during early- to mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was ongoing at the start of August 2022, with the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) at 0.35 feet above flood stage on August 1.  A significant amount of rural land was flooded until the river dropped below flood stage late on August 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite temperatures which averaged just a shade above normal, precipitation remained at 25 to 33 percent of normal at Platte and near Academy. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged 1 to 2 degrees above normal, while precipitation remained at 25 to 50 percent of normal near Centerville and Vermillion. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged around 2 degrees above normal at Mitchell during October, while precipitation was less than 25 percent of normal. Severe (D2) drought conditions  worsened to extreme (D3) conditions at mid-month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation near Forestburg and Woonsocket was around 10 percent of normal, with temperatures of 3 to 4 degrees above normal during September. Severe (D2) drought conditions developed through the final third of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 48 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites SD508 near Beresford, with a peak wind gust of 68 mph at 1312CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures averaged 2 to 3 degrees above normal at Alexandria during October, with precipitation around 25 percent of normal. Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of roof decking was removed on the corner of an apartment building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts badly damaged a metal outbuilding, which caused it to collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong deep-layer shear developed as a result of an increase in the low-level jet and turning mid-level winds ahead of the approaching strong upper-level trough. A strong push of mid-level instability created up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, despite an environment with a strong sub-cloud dry layer.  Storms moved rapidly northeast, with a cluster of hail producing storms in east central South Dakota. A second round of wind-producing storms erupted on the nose of the low-level jet across far northeast Nebraska, and translated quickly through southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota. |Strong synoptic winds were observed well to the north of the storms toward the southeast side of the Sioux Falls metro area with some reports of wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were overall fairly close to normal monthly means, precipitation was mainly above normal, and significantly so north of Interstate 90, where from 125 to 200 percent of normal precipitation was observed.  Areas mainly near and north of Interstate 90 had a small improvement in drought status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cool and wet period at the start of the month eased conditions from severe (D2) drought to moderate (D1) drought early in May.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous three-inch tree limbs were downed by winds well outside of thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
