EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD527 3 miles east-southeast of Forestburg measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 69 mph. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota. ||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple single family homes sustained significant damage to attached garages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak synoptic-scale wave progressed through the area atop a flat upper-level ridge, initiating several clusters of thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening.  Storms developed near a weak convergence zone, in an environment of 1000-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and marginally strong deep-layer shear of 30 knots.  Mainly multi-cell clusters resulted in marginally severe damaging wind gusts due to sub-cloud dryness and marginally large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple clusters of severe storms moved east and northeastward out of south central South Dakota, evolving into short bowing segments with damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon and evening of April 22, 2022 and moved northward with an advancing surface warm front. A couple thunderstorms took on supercell characteristics near the surface boundary and produced large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter. A brief tornado also occurred near Wolsey, SD.||In the post warm frontal air mass from mid-evening into the early overnight of April 23, 2022, non-thunderstorm high winds produced multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph across southeast South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed a 6-inch diameter limb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a compact jet streak ejected out of a deep western U.S. trough, a strong area of low pressure developed and tracked northeast from Kansas to Iowa.  Initial thermal profiles were warm enough to result in a brief period of light freezing rain before a transition to snow, but with generally light ice accumulations.  A few rumbles of thunder in far southeast South Dakota were accompanied by small hail.  A few hours of moderate to heavy snowfall occurred, especially areas west of the lower James River Valley which amassed the greatest snowfall totals around the area from 4 to 10 inches, with most other areas accumulating from 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.8 inches at Montrose and 5.0 inches at Bridgewater. Northwest wind gusts reached 30 to 40 mph and created a period of visibility around one-half mile during the late afternoon and evening of March 5.  A brief period of light freezing rain during the afternoon resulted in up to an eighth of an inch ice accumulation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches at Colton, 3.2 inches near Renner and 3.0 inches 6 miles south of Hartford. A brief period of light freezing rain in the afternoon resulted in up to one-tenth of an inch ice accumulation, and an isolated thunderstorm resulted in brief small hail and sleet. Northwest winds gusted to 35 to 40 mph and resulted in visibility less than one mile during the late afternoon and evening of March 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind increased to 25 to 35 mph, with numerous gusts to 45 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota following an overnight frontal passage.  With relative humidity levels from 15 to 25 percent during the afternoon, critical fire behavior occurred with several small rangeland wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 54 mph was measured at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD502 3 miles east-northeast of Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 6.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 1.9 miles south of Pukwana and 4.1 inches at the COOP site 5 miles south of Chamberlain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -52F 4 miles east-southeast of Parker (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD605).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to one-quarter to one-half inch on elevated and road surfaces near Madison, Orland, and at Ramona. Several large limbs up to 6 inches in diameter were broken due to ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common.  Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and increased wind by later on December 14 resulted in areas of blowing snow and heavy accumulation.  Visibility was as low as one-half mile at times, with a peak wind gust of 45 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 5 miles south of Arlington.  Snow accumulated to 10.2 inches at Iroquois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle began across much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day on Monday, December 12th, in response to an incoming area of strong low pressure. By the evening, a glaze of ice had formed on surfaces leading to hazardous travel conditions. Freezing rain then fell across and near the Prairie Coteau from Monday night into the day Tuesday, December 13th leading to significant ice accumulations of generally a quarter to half inch. Winds were easterly during the event, and gusted generally between 20 to 30 mph, occasionally up to 40 mph. Impacts were severe, including school closures, many vehicle accidents, tree damage, power outages involving over a thousand customers at any given time for multiple days, and the collapse of the KDLO transmitter tower near Garden City.||The ice storm was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a quarter to half inch of ice accumulated on surfaces across the southwestern portions of the county.

A series of Arctic air masses crossed the region over  period of 6 days beginning on Sunday, December 18th. Temperatures failed to even reach 5 degrees above zero during this period, with temperatures consistently dropping into the teens below zero at night. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 3 of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing snow pack. High temperatures on the 22nd were as cold as -12 F in Watertown and -10 F in Aberdeen and Mobridge. Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions for many. The highest recorded wind gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd. The coldest measured wind chills from the 21st into the 22nd include -61 F at Trail City, -59 F at Eureka and 10 miles south of Bullhead, -58 F at Timber Lake, -57 F at McIntosh and 5 miles SE of Selby, and -56 F at Vivian, Foster Bay, Craven, Mac's Corner, Agar, Herreid, and Bowdle; the coldest on the 23rd include -52 F at Eureka, and -50 F at Trail City and 10 miles south of Bulhead.||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the SDDOT. Numerous vehicle accidents and rescues occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 5.7 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 10.2 miles west of Dimock and 4.8 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 0.1 miles south-southeast of Corsica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status improved slightly in some areas, but was largely unchanged across southeast South Dakota given frozen ground conditions. Many areas received double to triple the amount of normal precipitation for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought improved to moderate (D1) by late December. Monthly precipitation at Howard was around 300 percent of normal, most in the form of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status improved slightly in some areas, but was largely unchanged across southeast South Dakota given frozen ground conditions. Many areas received double to triple the amount of normal precipitation for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through December. Monthly precipitation at Tyndall was around 150 of normal, most in the form of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -56F near White Lake (South Dakota State University Mesonet site WTES2) and -54F 4 miles west of Plankinton (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to one-quarter to one-half inch on elevated and road surfaces at Brookings, Bruce, and near Elkton. Several large limbs up to 6 to 8 inches in diameter were broken due to ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common.  Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and increased wind by early on December 15 resulted in areas of blowing snow and heavy accumulation.  Visibility was as low as one-half mile at times, with a peak wind gust of 48 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD597 near Howard.  Snow accumulated to 7.9 inches at Howard.

Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions for many. The highest recorded wind gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd. The coldest measured wind chills from the 21st into the 22nd include -61 F at Trail City, -59 F at Eureka and 10 miles south of Bullhead, -58 F at Timber Lake, -57 F at McIntosh and 5 miles SE of Selby, and -56 F at Vivian, Foster Bay, Craven, Mac's Corner, Agar, Herreid, and Bowdle; the coldest on the 23rd include -52 F at Eureka, and -50 F at Trail City and 10 miles south of Bulhead.||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the SDDOT. Numerous vehicle accidents and rescues occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.

A strong area of low pressure brought strong winds and periods of snow and heavy snow over a 4 plus day period. The bulk of the snow across central South Dakota fell between 6am on December 13th through 6am on December 14th, with more variability across northeastern South Dakota. Some of the highest snowfall totals by December 16th include 23 inches 4 miles north of Victor, 22.5 inches 2.6 miles NW of Okaton, 22 inches 14 miles NNE of Isabel and in Kennebec, 21 inches in Faulkton, 20.5 inches 7.1 miles WNW of Hecla, and 20 inches in Highmore and 8.3 miles SSW of Seneca. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday December 15th, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on December 13th through mid-day December 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm December 14th through 9am December 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, school was cancelled for multiple consecutive days at numerous locations.||The snow and heavy snow was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th-16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow had been recorded by 9am on the 13th in Murdo. The storm total snowfall there by 9am on the 17th was 19.2 inches.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 8.0 inches at the COOP site in Bridgewater, 7.0 inches at the COOP site 5 miles northeast of Salem, 7.5 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 5.8 miles northwest of Montrose, and 6.0 inches at the COOP site in Montrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -52F 10 miles west of Platte (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD554) and -50F 5 miles west of Geddes (RAWS site RLAS2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common.  Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and increased wind by early on December 15 resulted in areas of blowing snow and heavy accumulation.  Visibility was as low as one-half mile at times, with a peak wind gust of 45 mph at South Dakota State University Mesonet site PSTS2 at Parkston.  Snow accumulated to 10.2 inches near Clayton and 5.5 inches at Menno (COOP site MNOS2).

Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and increased wind resulted in brief visibility as low as around 1 mile near Tyndall. Wind gusted as high as 52 mph 5 miles east-southeast of Tyndall at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD571, with snowfall of 4.5 inches at Tyndall (COOP site TYNS2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 1 to 3 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of southeast South Dakota on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended. Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. |Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 53 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD547 near Montrose, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

Heavy snow of at least 6 inches in 12 hours was recorded from December 15th into the 16th in conjunction with the blizzard conditions across Marshall, Day, Codington, Grant and Roberts Counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of only slight improvement in visibility took place during the overnights.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 7.4 inches at the COOP site (KFSD) in Sioux Falls, 10.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 6.9 miles west of Garretson, 8.1 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 0.5 miles north of Hartford, and 10.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 6.4 miles south of Hartford. Other reports around the Sioux Falls metropolitan area generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

Other reports around the Sioux Falls metropolitan area generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status improved slightly in some areas, but was largely unchanged across southeast South Dakota given frozen ground conditions. Many areas received double to triple the amount of normal precipitation for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through December. Monthly precipitation at Platte and near Academy was around 300 percent of normal, most in the form of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -56F 1 mile east-southeast of Dallas (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD602), -54F 4 miles east of Dixon (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD589), -52F 2 miles west-southwest of Pickstown (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD588), and -51F at Gregory (site K9D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common.  Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early on December 14, but winds did not increase until early on December 15.  Consistent visibility of one-quarter mile or less occurred for more than a day, with a peak wind gust reaching 55 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System site KHON in Huron, and 53 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Wolsey.  Snowfall totaled 17.0 inches near Wessington (COOP site WESS2) and 13.4 inches at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 1 to 3 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of southeast South Dakota on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended. Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. |Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 49 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD519 10 miles east of Davis, and 39 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site K7G9 at Canton, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought strong winds and periods of snow and heavy snow over a 4 plus day period. The bulk of the snow across central South Dakota fell between 6am on December 13th through 6am on December 14th, with more variability across northeastern South Dakota. Some of the highest snowfall totals by December 16th include 23 inches 4 miles north of Victor, 22.5 inches 2.6 miles NW of Okaton, 22 inches 14 miles NNE of Isabel and in Kennebec, 21 inches in Faulkton, 20.5 inches 7.1 miles WNW of Hecla, and 20 inches in Highmore and 8.3 miles SSW of Seneca. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday December 15th, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on December 13th through mid-day December 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm December 14th through 9am December 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, school was cancelled for multiple consecutive days at numerous locations.||The snow and heavy snow was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th-16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was recorded by 7am on the 14th in Onida. Additional accumulating snow fell thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced snow and heavy snow prior to the onset of strong northwesterly winds and periods of additional snow, which resulted in blizzard or ground blizzard conditions across much of central and northeastern South Dakota for extended periods of time from the morning of December 14th through the afternoon of December 16th. Heavy snow of at least 6 inches in 12 hours was recorded from December 15th into the 16th in conjunction with the blizzard conditions across Marshall, Day, Codington, Grant and Roberts Counties. Winds gusted generally between 45 and 60 mph, with the highest recorded gusts including 63 mph at Pierre, 61 mph 10 miles SW of the Chantler Creek Rec Area, 59 mph 3 miles N of Gann Valley, and 58 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on Tue Dec 13th through mid-day Sat Dec 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm Wed Dec 14th through 9am Sat Dec 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, power outages were reported across the area, and school was cancelled at numerous locations for multiple consecutive days. ||The blizzard was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of only slight improvement in visibility took place during the overnights.

An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 inches of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing loose snow pack (highest amounts include 4 inches 2 miles NW of Big Stone City, 3.5 inches 6 miles SW of Gary, 3.3 inches 2 miles SW of McLaughlin). Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from December 21st through December 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions (highest gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd).||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was virtually shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation. I29 closed from 6pm CST on December 22nd from Sioux Falls to the ND state line until 9am on December 23rd from Sioux Falls to Watertown and until noon from Watertown to the ND state line, and I90 closed from Rapid City to Chamberlain from 6pm MST on December 21st until 2:30pm on December 23rd from Rapid City to Murdo and until 4pm from Murdo to Mitchell. Additional impacts included numerous vehicle accidents and rescues, as well as numerous school closures. Governor Noem declared a Winter Storm Emergency on December 22nd, which activated the SD National Guard and allowed assistance from the state to county governments as needed. Furthermore, a Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

I29 closed from 6pm CST on December 22nd from Sioux Falls to the ND state line until 9am on December 23rd from Sioux Falls to Watertown and until noon from Watertown to the ND state line, and I90 closed from Rapid City to Chamberlain from 6pm MST on December 21st until 2:30pm on December 23rd from Rapid City to Murdo and until 4pm from Murdo to Mitchell. Additional impacts included numerous vehicle accidents and rescues, as well as numerous school closures. Governor Noem declared a Winter Storm Emergency on December 22nd, which activated the SD National Guard and allowed assistance from the state to county governments as needed. Furthermore, a Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 7.0 inches at the COOP site in Vermillion and 3.5 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 1.4 miles southeast of Vermillion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status improved slightly in some areas, but was largely unchanged across southeast South Dakota given frozen ground conditions. Many areas received double to triple the amount of normal precipitation for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought improved to severe (D2) by late December. Monthly precipitation at White Lake was between 250 and 300 percent of normal, most in the form of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -48F 4 miles south of Arlington (South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD504).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common.  Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early on December 14, but winds did not increase until early on December 15.  Consistent visibility of one-quarter mile occurred for more than a day, with a peak wind gust reaching 40 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site K9V9 in Chamberlain, and 50 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD537 near Kimball.  Snowfall totaled 15.0 inches at Chamberlain, 14.0 inches near Pukwana, and 12.3 inches 7 miles south-southwest of Chamberlain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 1 to 3 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of southeast South Dakota on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended. Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. |Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 51 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD508 near Beresford and 46 mph at site SD550 near North Sioux City, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced snow and heavy snow prior to the onset of strong northwesterly winds and periods of additional snow, which resulted in blizzard or ground blizzard conditions across much of central and northeastern South Dakota for extended periods of time from the morning of December 14th through the afternoon of December 16th. Heavy snow of at least 6 inches in 12 hours was recorded from December 15th into the 16th in conjunction with the blizzard conditions across Marshall, Day, Codington, Grant and Roberts Counties. Winds gusted generally between 45 and 60 mph, with the highest recorded gusts including 63 mph at Pierre, 61 mph 10 miles SW of the Chantler Creek Rec Area, 59 mph 3 miles N of Gann Valley, and 58 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on Tue Dec 13th through mid-day Sat Dec 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm Wed Dec 14th through 9am Sat Dec 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, power outages were reported across the area, and school was cancelled at numerous locations for multiple consecutive days. ||The blizzard was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common.  Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa.  Travel was severely impacted with Interstate 90 closed west of Mitchell, with no travel advised for most other roads from the James River Valley westward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed very early on December 14, but winds did not increase until late on December 14.  Consistent visibility of one-quarter mile occurred for more than a day, with a peak wind gust reaching 50 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System sites SD602 near Dallas and SD589 near Dixon.  Snowfall totaled 16.5 inches at Gregory (COOP site GRGS2), 13.0 inches near Burke, and 9.5 inches 4 miles south-southwest of Pickstown (COOP site PCKS2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 inches of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing loose snow pack (highest amounts include 4 inches 2 miles NW of Big Stone City, 3.5 inches 6 miles SW of Gary, 3.3 inches 2 miles SW of McLaughlin). Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from December 21st through December 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions (highest gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd).||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was virtually shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation. I29 closed from 6pm CST on December 22nd from Sioux Falls to the ND state line until 9am on December 23rd from Sioux Falls to Watertown and until noon from Watertown to the ND state line, and I90 closed from Rapid City to Chamberlain from 6pm MST on December 21st until 2:30pm on December 23rd from Rapid City to Murdo and until 4pm from Murdo to Mitchell. Additional impacts included numerous vehicle accidents and rescues, as well as numerous school closures. Governor Noem declared a Winter Storm Emergency on December 22nd, which activated the SD National Guard and allowed assistance from the state to county governments as needed. Furthermore, a Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 5.6 inches at the COOP site 2 miles southeast of Madison.

After a 1 to 3 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of southeast South Dakota on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended. Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. |Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 47 mph at both South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD553 near Plankinton and South Dakota State University Mesonet site WTES2 at White Lake, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

Most county, state, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended. Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. |Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 48 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD603 near Lane, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Arctic air masses crossed the region over  period of 6 days beginning on Sunday, December 18th. Temperatures failed to even reach 5 degrees above zero during this period, with temperatures consistently dropping into the teens below zero at night. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 3 of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing snow pack. High temperatures on the 22nd were as cold as -12 F in Watertown and -10 F in Aberdeen and Mobridge. Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions for many. The highest recorded wind gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd. The coldest measured wind chills from the 21st into the 22nd include -61 F at Trail City, -59 F at Eureka and 10 miles south of Bullhead, -58 F at Timber Lake, -57 F at McIntosh and 5 miles SE of Selby, and -56 F at Vivian, Foster Bay, Craven, Mac's Corner, Agar, Herreid, and Bowdle; the coldest on the 23rd include -52 F at Eureka, and -50 F at Trail City and 10 miles south of Bulhead.||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the SDDOT. Numerous vehicle accidents and rescues occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.

A strong area of low pressure brought strong winds and periods of snow and heavy snow over a 4 plus day period. The bulk of the snow across central South Dakota fell between 6am on December 13th through 6am on December 14th, with more variability across northeastern South Dakota. Some of the highest snowfall totals by December 16th include 23 inches 4 miles north of Victor, 22.5 inches 2.6 miles NW of Okaton, 22 inches 14 miles NNE of Isabel and in Kennebec, 21 inches in Faulkton, 20.5 inches 7.1 miles WNW of Hecla, and 20 inches in Highmore and 8.3 miles SSW of Seneca. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday December 15th, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on December 13th through mid-day December 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm December 14th through 9am December 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, school was cancelled for multiple consecutive days at numerous locations.||The snow and heavy snow was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th-16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 9 inches of snow was recorded by 7am on the 14th 8.3 miles SSW of Seneca. The storm total snowfall there by 6pm on the 16th was 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 3.5 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 5.6 miles north-northeast of Elk Point. Several vehicles slid into the ditch along Interstate 29 due to slick road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle began across much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day on Monday, December 12th, in response to an incoming area of strong low pressure. By the evening, a glaze of ice had formed on surfaces leading to hazardous travel conditions. Freezing rain then fell across and near the Prairie Coteau from Monday night into the day Tuesday, December 13th leading to significant ice accumulations of generally a quarter to half inch. Winds were easterly during the event, and gusted generally between 20 to 30 mph, occasionally up to 40 mph. Impacts were severe, including school closures, many vehicle accidents, tree damage, power outages involving over a thousand customers at any given time for multiple days, and the collapse of the KDLO transmitter tower near Garden City.||The ice storm was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced snow and heavy snow prior to the onset of strong northwesterly winds and periods of additional snow, which resulted in blizzard or ground blizzard conditions across much of central and northeastern South Dakota for extended periods of time from the morning of December 14th through the afternoon of December 16th. Heavy snow of at least 6 inches in 12 hours was recorded from December 15th into the 16th in conjunction with the blizzard conditions across Marshall, Day, Codington, Grant and Roberts Counties. Winds gusted generally between 45 and 60 mph, with the highest recorded gusts including 63 mph at Pierre, 61 mph 10 miles SW of the Chantler Creek Rec Area, 59 mph 3 miles N of Gann Valley, and 58 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on Tue Dec 13th through mid-day Sat Dec 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm Wed Dec 14th through 9am Sat Dec 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, power outages were reported across the area, and school was cancelled at numerous locations for multiple consecutive days. ||The blizzard was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 inches of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing loose snow pack (highest amounts include 4 inches 2 miles NW of Big Stone City, 3.5 inches 6 miles SW of Gary, 3.3 inches 2 miles SW of McLaughlin). Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from December 21st through December 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions (highest gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd).||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was virtually shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation. I29 closed from 6pm CST on December 22nd from Sioux Falls to the ND state line until 9am on December 23rd from Sioux Falls to Watertown and until noon from Watertown to the ND state line, and I90 closed from Rapid City to Chamberlain from 6pm MST on December 21st until 2:30pm on December 23rd from Rapid City to Murdo and until 4pm from Murdo to Mitchell. Additional impacts included numerous vehicle accidents and rescues, as well as numerous school closures. Governor Noem declared a Winter Storm Emergency on December 22nd, which activated the SD National Guard and allowed assistance from the state to county governments as needed. Furthermore, a Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to around one-quarter inch on elevated and road surfaces around Sioux Falls.  Slick conditions resulted in several accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 3.0 inches at the COOP site 2 miles east of Yankton and 2.4 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 0.3 miles east-northeast of Yankton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle began across much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day on Monday, December 12th, in response to an incoming area of strong low pressure. By the evening, a glaze of ice had formed on surfaces leading to hazardous travel conditions. Freezing rain then fell across and near the Prairie Coteau from Monday night into the day Tuesday, December 13th leading to significant ice accumulations of generally a quarter to half inch. Winds were easterly during the event, and gusted generally between 20 to 30 mph, occasionally up to 40 mph. Impacts were severe, including school closures, many vehicle accidents, tree damage, power outages involving over a thousand customers at any given time for multiple days, and the collapse of the KDLO transmitter tower near Garden City.||The ice storm was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a quarter to half inch of ice accumulated on surfaces across roughly the eastern two thirds of the county. The KDLO transmitter tower near Garden City collapsed due to the weight of the ice and breezy conditions.

A series of Arctic air masses crossed the region over  period of 6 days beginning on Sunday, December 18th. Temperatures failed to even reach 5 degrees above zero during this period, with temperatures consistently dropping into the teens below zero at night. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 3 of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing snow pack. High temperatures on the 22nd were as cold as -12 F in Watertown and -10 F in Aberdeen and Mobridge. Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions for many. The highest recorded wind gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd. The coldest measured wind chills from the 21st into the 22nd include -61 F at Trail City, -59 F at Eureka and 10 miles south of Bullhead, -58 F at Timber Lake, -57 F at McIntosh and 5 miles SE of Selby, and -56 F at Vivian, Foster Bay, Craven, Mac's Corner, Agar, Herreid, and Bowdle; the coldest on the 23rd include -52 F at Eureka, and -50 F at Trail City and 10 miles south of Bulhead.||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the SDDOT. Numerous vehicle accidents and rescues occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.

A strong area of low pressure brought strong winds and periods of snow and heavy snow over a 4 plus day period. The bulk of the snow across central South Dakota fell between 6am on December 13th through 6am on December 14th, with more variability across northeastern South Dakota. Some of the highest snowfall totals by December 16th include 23 inches 4 miles north of Victor, 22.5 inches 2.6 miles NW of Okaton, 22 inches 14 miles NNE of Isabel and in Kennebec, 21 inches in Faulkton, 20.5 inches 7.1 miles WNW of Hecla, and 20 inches in Highmore and 8.3 miles SSW of Seneca. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday December 15th, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on December 13th through mid-day December 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm December 14th through 9am December 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, school was cancelled for multiple consecutive days at numerous locations.||The snow and heavy snow was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th-16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was recorded by 10pm on the 13th in Sisseton. The storm total snowfall there by 10pm on the 16th was 17.7 inches.

A strong low pressure system produced snow and heavy snow prior to the onset of strong northwesterly winds and periods of additional snow, which resulted in blizzard or ground blizzard conditions across much of central and northeastern South Dakota for extended periods of time from the morning of December 14th through the afternoon of December 16th. Heavy snow of at least 6 inches in 12 hours was recorded from December 15th into the 16th in conjunction with the blizzard conditions across Marshall, Day, Codington, Grant and Roberts Counties. Winds gusted generally between 45 and 60 mph, with the highest recorded gusts including 63 mph at Pierre, 61 mph 10 miles SW of the Chantler Creek Rec Area, 59 mph 3 miles N of Gann Valley, and 58 mph 3 miles SE of Reliance. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday, as numerous roads had become impassable. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 inches of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing loose snow pack (highest amounts include 4 inches 2 miles NW of Big Stone City, 3.5 inches 6 miles SW of Gary, 3.3 inches 2 miles SW of McLaughlin). Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from December 21st through December 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions (highest gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd).||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was virtually shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation. I29 closed from 6pm CST on December 22nd from Sioux Falls to the ND state line until 9am on December 23rd from Sioux Falls to Watertown and until noon from Watertown to the ND state line, and I90 closed from Rapid City to Chamberlain from 6pm MST on December 21st until 2:30pm on December 23rd from Rapid City to Murdo and until 4pm from Murdo to Mitchell. Additional impacts included numerous vehicle accidents and rescues, as well as numerous school closures. Governor Noem declared a Winter Storm Emergency on December 22nd, which activated the SD National Guard and allowed assistance from the state to county governments as needed. Furthermore, a Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to around one-half inch on elevated surfaces at Parker.  Slick conditions resulted in several accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated from one-quarter to one-half inch on elevated surfaces at Woonsocket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 4.0 inches at the COOP site 2 miles northeast of Academy and 3.0 inches at the COOP site in Platte.

Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 6.5 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 8.7 miles north of Gregory, 3.0 inches at the COOP site in Gregory, 3.0 inches at the COOP site 4 miles south-southwest of Pickstown, and 2.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 4.2 miles southwest of Burke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle began across much of central and northeastern South Dakota during the day on Monday, December 12th, in response to an incoming area of strong low pressure. By the evening, a glaze of ice had formed on surfaces leading to hazardous travel conditions. Freezing rain then fell across and near the Prairie Coteau from Monday night into the day Tuesday, December 13th leading to significant ice accumulations of generally a quarter to half inch. Winds were easterly during the event, and gusted generally between 20 to 30 mph, occasionally up to 40 mph. Impacts were severe, including school closures, many vehicle accidents, tree damage, power outages involving over a thousand customers at any given time for multiple days, and the collapse of the KDLO transmitter tower near Garden City.||The ice storm was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th through 16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Arctic air masses crossed the region over  period of 6 days beginning on Sunday, December 18th. Temperatures failed to even reach 5 degrees above zero during this period, with temperatures consistently dropping into the teens below zero at night. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 3 of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing snow pack. High temperatures on the 22nd were as cold as -12 F in Watertown and -10 F in Aberdeen and Mobridge. Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions for many. The highest recorded wind gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd. The coldest measured wind chills from the 21st into the 22nd include -61 F at Trail City, -59 F at Eureka and 10 miles south of Bullhead, -58 F at Timber Lake, -57 F at McIntosh and 5 miles SE of Selby, and -56 F at Vivian, Foster Bay, Craven, Mac's Corner, Agar, Herreid, and Bowdle; the coldest on the 23rd include -52 F at Eureka, and -50 F at Trail City and 10 miles south of Bulhead.||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the SDDOT. Numerous vehicle accidents and rescues occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.

A strong area of low pressure brought strong winds and periods of snow and heavy snow over a 4 plus day period. The bulk of the snow across central South Dakota fell between 6am on December 13th through 6am on December 14th, with more variability across northeastern South Dakota. Some of the highest snowfall totals by December 16th include 23 inches 4 miles north of Victor, 22.5 inches 2.6 miles NW of Okaton, 22 inches 14 miles NNE of Isabel and in Kennebec, 21 inches in Faulkton, 20.5 inches 7.1 miles WNW of Hecla, and 20 inches in Highmore and 8.3 miles SSW of Seneca. ||The South Dakota Department of Transportation placed nearly the entire state under No Travel Advised or had road closures by Thursday December 15th, as numerous roads had become impassable. I90 closed from Chamberlain to Rapid City from 10am CST on December 13th through mid-day December 17th (from Kadoka to Chamberlain), and I29 closed from Watertown to the ND border from 7pm December 14th through 9am December 17th. Several dozens of semi drivers were stranded for consecutive days and nights at the Coffee Cup Fuel Stop in Vivian, and numerous other vehicle accidents and rescues occurred as well. Additionally, school was cancelled for multiple consecutive days at numerous locations.||The snow and heavy snow was just one component of a highly impactful, major winter storm. This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, and produced freezing rain, heavy snow and/or blizzard conditions from December 12th-16th across the region. A Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was recorded by 7am on the 14th 3 miles NW of Westport. The storm total snowfall there by 7am on the 16th was 14.0 inches.

An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 inches of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing loose snow pack (highest amounts include 4 inches 2 miles NW of Big Stone City, 3.5 inches 6 miles SW of Gary, 3.3 inches 2 miles SW of McLaughlin). Wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph behind this front impacted the region from December 21st through December 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -60 degree F range and ground blizzard conditions (highest gusts include 60 mph at Hayes, 58 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead, 54 mph at Vivian, and 53 mph at Agar and Pierre all on the 22nd).||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads became impassable. Nearly the entire state was virtually shut down, for the second time this December, as roads were either deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the South Dakota Department of Transportation. I29 closed from 6pm CST on December 22nd from Sioux Falls to the ND state line until 9am on December 23rd from Sioux Falls to Watertown and until noon from Watertown to the ND state line, and I90 closed from Rapid City to Chamberlain from 6pm MST on December 21st until 2:30pm on December 23rd from Rapid City to Murdo and until 4pm from Murdo to Mitchell. Additional impacts included numerous vehicle accidents and rescues, as well as numerous school closures. Governor Noem declared a Winter Storm Emergency on December 22nd, which activated the SD National Guard and allowed assistance from the state to county governments as needed. Furthermore, a Major Disaster Declaration was declared on February 27th by Governor Noem for several counties across central and northeastern South Dakota for winter weather from December 12-25th.

The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer.  With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southeast South Dakota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to one to two tenths of an inch on elevated surfaces near Tea and Lennox.
